  <path d="M78 326 v8 q0 6 6 6 h106 q6 0 6 6 q0 -6 6 -6 h102 q6 0 6 -6 v-8" fill="none" stroke="var(--gold-deep)" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="220" y="364" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="700" fill="var(--gold-deep)">front : rear  =  8 : 15</text>
</svg>
blocks.cap: Two ratios that share a quantity, \(4:3\) for front to middle and \(2:5\) for middle to rear, do not line up while their shared part is written as \(3\) in one and \(2\) in the other. Scaling the first by \(2\) and the second by \(3\) turns both middles into \(6\), so the bars snap together into \(8:6:15\) and you can read off \(8:15\) for front to rear. Matching the shared part is the whole trick that lets two ratios join into one.

blocks.x: A paint studio mixes a custom shade in two stages. The first recipe compares yellow to blue as \(3:2\). A second recipe compares blue to white as \(4:7\). Blue is the color both recipes share, but it shows up as \(2\) in the first and as \(4\) in the second, so the two recipes are not speaking the same language yet. Rescale one recipe so the blue numbers agree, then line all three colors up as yellow to blue to white. What is the ratio of yellow to white, written as \(a:b\) in lowest terms?
hints: Blue is \(2\) in the first ratio and \(4\) in the second. To make them match, double everything in the first ratio so its blue becomes \(4\). What does \(3:2\) become when you double both parts?

After doubling, yellow to blue is \(6:4\) and blue to white is \(4:7\). The blue \(4\) is now the same in both, so stack them into one chain \(6:4:7\). Read off just the yellow and the white parts, then reduce.
blocks.sol: The shared color is blue, but it carries different numbers in the two recipes, so first make those numbers agree. Blue is \(2\) in the yellow to blue ratio and \(4\) in the blue to white ratio. Doubling the first ratio turns its blue into \(4\) without changing the comparison, since multiplying both parts by the same number gives an equivalent ratio. So \(3:2\) becomes \(6:4\). The second ratio already has blue as \(4\), so leave it as \(4:7\). Now both recipes agree that blue is \(4\), and you can line all three colors up in one chain as yellow to blue to white, \(6:4:7\). Reading off only the first and last parts gives yellow to white as \(6:7\). The greatest common factor of \(6\) and \(7\) is \(1\), so this is already in lowest terms. The ratio of yellow to white is \(\boxed{6:7}\).

blocks.x: Strike a major chord on a piano and it sounds settled, almost like it has come home, and a big reason is hiding in a ratio with three parts. The three notes of the chord, the root, the major third, and the fifth, vibrate at frequencies that line up very close to \(4:5:6\). Tucked right inside that comparison is one of the small relationships people have loved for ages, the fifth above the root, since the root and the fifth give \(4:6\), which is just \(3:2\). The octave, the other famously consonant pairing at \(2:1\), is the next building block your ear leans on, and stacking those tidy whole-number relationships is part of why the chord rings as consonant to us. The parts \(4\), \(5\), and \(6\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\), so \(4:5:6\) is already in lowest terms. It is just a three-way ratio doing real work in the world, the same kind of comparison you are learning to read and simplify in this lesson.

A sourdough baker keeps her recipe in two separate notes. One note says the flour to water ratio is \(5:3\). The other note says the water to starter ratio is \(6:5\). Water is the ingredient both notes mention, but it shows up as \(3\) in the first and as \(6\) in the second, so the two notes do not yet speak the same language. Once you rescale them so the water amounts agree, the three ingredients fold into a single flour to water to starter ratio. The baker mixes one batch using exactly these comparisons, and the three ingredients together weigh \(630\) grams. How many grams of flour are in the batch?
hints: The water amount is 3 in the first ratio and 6 in the second. Rescale one ratio so both read the same number for water. Doubling \(5:3\) gives \(10:6\), which now matches the \(6\) in the water to starter ratio, so you can stitch them into one flour to water to starter ratio.
Once the three-way ratio is \(10:6:5\), add the parts to see how many equal shares the \(630\) grams splits into, find what one share weighs, then take the flour parts.
blocks.sol: The two ratios share water, but they disagree on its number. Flour to water is \(5:3\) and water to starter is \(6:5\). To link them, rescale the first ratio so its water matches the \(6\) in the second. Multiplying \(5:3\) by \(2\) gives \(10:6\), so flour to water is \(10:6\) and water to starter is \(6:5\). Now both call water \(6\), and the three line up into one ratio, \(10:6:5\). The greatest common factor of \(10\), \(6\), and \(5\) is \(1\), so it is already in lowest terms. Add the parts to get \(10+6+5=21\) equal shares. The batch weighs \(630\) grams, so one share weighs \(\tfrac{630}{21}=30\) grams. Flour is \(10\) shares, which is \(10\times 30 = 300\) grams. As a check, water is \(6\times 30 = 180\) and starter is \(5\times 30 = 150\), and \(300+180+150 = 630\). So the batch contains \(\boxed{300}\) grams of flour.

review.x: A backyard birdseed blend follows a fixed recipe. For every \(7\) parts millet there are \(4\) parts sunflower and \(2\) parts cracked corn, which we can write as the multi-way ratio \(7:4:2\) in that order. Suppose you mix exactly one full unscaled batch, meaning you use the recipe numbers just as they are written without scaling them up or down. How many parts of birdseed do you end up with in total? Type the single number.
hints: A multi-way ratio like \(7:4:2\) hides a total inside it. The whole blend is made of all of its parts added together.
Add the three part numbers, \(7\), \(4\), and \(2\). One unscaled batch means you do not multiply any of them first.
review.sol: A multi-way ratio carries a hidden total, the sum of all its parts. Here the blend is \(7\) parts millet, \(4\) parts sunflower, and \(2\) parts cracked corn. One full unscaled batch uses those numbers exactly as written, so no rescaling is needed and each part keeps its given size. Add every part to find the whole, \(7 + 4 + 2 = 13\). So one batch is \(\boxed{13}\) parts of birdseed in total.

review.x: A printmaker keeps a notebook of every ink blend she uses. One favourite blend is logged as the ratio \(20:50:35\), cyan to magenta to yellow, in drops. She wants to record it in lowest terms so the same recipe written in any size still matches. Divide every part by the greatest common factor of all three parts so the results share no factor larger than \(1\). Write the simplified ratio in the form \(a:b:c\).
hints: To simplify a multi-way ratio you divide every part by the same number, the greatest common factor of all three parts. List the factors of \(20\), \(50\), and \(35\) and find the largest one they all share.

All three parts end in \(0\) or \(5\), so they are all multiples of \(5\). Check whether anything bigger than \(5\) divides all three. It does not, since \(35\) is not even, so \(5\) is the greatest common factor. Divide each part by \(5\).
review.sol: First find the greatest common factor of all three parts. The factors of \(20\) are \(1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20\), the factors of \(50\) are \(1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50\), and the factors of \(35\) are \(1, 5, 7, 35\). The largest number on all three lists is \(5\), so the greatest common factor is \(5\). Now divide every part by \(5\). You get \(20 \div 5 = 4\), then \(50 \div 5 = 10\), then \(35 \div 5 = 7\). That gives \(4:10:7\). Check that this is fully reduced. The parts are \(4\), \(10\), and \(7\), and \(7\) is prime and does not divide \(4\) or \(10\), so the three parts share no common factor larger than \(1\). The blend in lowest terms is \(\boxed{4:10:7}\).

review.x: A small batch of homemade trail mix is built around a fixed comparison of oats, raisins, and seeds, written as the ratio \(10:9:25\). Before measuring out a giant festival batch, the maker wants the cleanest possible form of this comparison, so she checks whether all three numbers share a common factor she could divide out. Reduce \(10:9:25\) to lowest terms by dividing every part by the greatest common factor of all three numbers, and if nothing divides cleanly into all three, then the ratio you started with already is the answer. Write your answer in the form a:b:c.
hints: To reduce a multi-way ratio you need the greatest common factor of all three parts at once, not just two of them. A number only counts if it divides \(10\), \(9\), and \(25\) without leaving a remainder. Notice that \(9\) is odd, so \(2\) is out, and \(9\) is not a multiple of \(5\), so \(5\) is out too.

Check the factors of \(9\), which are \(1\), \(3\), and \(9\). Does \(3\) divide \(10\)? Does \(9\) divide \(10\)? If the only number that divides all three parts is \(1\), the ratio is already in lowest terms and you leave it exactly as it is.
review.sol: To reduce a multi-way ratio you divide every part by the greatest common factor of all three numbers together, so the real task is to find what \(10\), \(9\), and \(25\) share. Look at \(9\) first, since it is the most restrictive. It is odd, so \(2\) cannot divide it, which already rules out any even common factor. Its only factors are \(1\), \(3\), and \(9\). Now test \(3\) against the others. It does not divide \(10\), since \(10=3\times 3+1\), so \(3\) is not a common factor. The same goes for \(9\). That leaves only \(1\) as a number dividing all three parts, so the greatest common factor of \(10\), \(9\), and \(25\) is \(1\). When the greatest common factor is \(1\), there is nothing to divide out, and the ratio is already as small as it can get. Dividing each part by \(1\) just gives back \(10:9:25\), so the comparison was already in lowest terms from the start. \(\boxed{10:9:25}\)

review.x: A flower farmer fills a long border bed using a fixed planting comparison of marigolds to basil to thyme. In one stretch of the bed she counts \(42\) marigolds, \(14\) basil plants, and \(28\) thyme plants, written as the multi-way ratio \(42:14:28\). Put this comparison in lowest terms by dividing every part by the greatest common factor of all three parts. Write your answer in the form \(a:b:c\).
hints: A multi-way ratio is in lowest terms when the greatest common factor of all three parts is \(1\). Start by listing the factors of each part and finding the largest number that divides \(42\), \(14\), and \(28\) all at once.
Each part is even and each is a multiple of \(7\), so try dividing every part by \(14\). Keep the order the same: do \(42\div 14\), then \(14\div 14\), then \(28\div 14\).
review.sol: To reduce a multi-way ratio I divide every part by the greatest common factor of all the parts, not just two of them. The parts are \(42\), \(14\), and \(28\). The factors of \(14\) are \(1, 2, 7, 14\), and the largest of these that also divides \(42\) and \(28\) is \(14\) itself, since \(42=14\times 3\) and \(28=14\times 2\). So the greatest common factor of all three is \(14\). Now I divide each part by \(14\), keeping the order marigolds, basil, thyme. That gives \(42\div 14=3\), then \(14\div 14=1\), then \(28\div 14=2\), so the ratio becomes \(3:1:2\). I can check by scaling back up, since \(3\times 14:1\times 14:2\times 14=42:14:28\), the original. The new parts \(3\), \(1\), and \(2\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\), so this is fully reduced. \(\boxed{3:1:2}\)

review.x: A breakfast smoothie is built from \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) cup mango, \(\tfrac{1}{6}\) cup spinach, and \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) cup yogurt, written in that order as \(\tfrac{1}{3} : \tfrac{1}{6} : \tfrac{1}{2}\). Scale all three parts up by the same whole number to clear the fractions, then reduce. What is the simplified ratio of mango to spinach to yogurt in the form a:b:c?
hints: The three denominators are 3, 6, and 2. Find one number that all of them divide into evenly, then multiply every part by it so the fractions disappear.
The smallest number that 3, 6, and 2 all divide into is 6. Multiply each cup amount by 6 to get whole numbers, then check whether those whole numbers share a common factor you can divide out.
review.sol: To compare these fairly, the cup amounts need to be whole numbers. The denominators are 3, 6, and 2, and the smallest number all three divide into evenly is 6. Multiply every part by 6. Mango becomes \(\tfrac{1}{3}\times 6 = 2\), spinach becomes \(\tfrac{1}{6}\times 6 = 1\), and yogurt becomes \(\tfrac{1}{2}\times 6 = 3\). Multiplying all three parts by the same number keeps the ratio equivalent, so \(\tfrac{1}{3} : \tfrac{1}{6} : \tfrac{1}{2}\) is the same as \(2:1:3\). Now check for a common factor across all three parts. The greatest common factor of 2, 1, and 3 is 1, so the ratio is already in lowest terms. The simplified ratio of mango to spinach to yogurt is \(\boxed{2:1:3}\).

review.x: A small studio mixes a signature candle from \(2\tfrac{1}{2}\) ounces of soy wax, \(1\tfrac{1}{4}\) ounces of beeswax, and \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) ounce of fragrance oil. To compare these cleanly, scale every part up by the same whole number until the fractions disappear, then divide out the common factor. Written in lowest terms as \(a:b:c\), what is the ratio of soy wax to beeswax to fragrance oil?
hints: Every part is a number of eighths. Multiply each part by 8 to clear all the fractions at once, and you get three whole numbers.
After scaling you have \(20:10:5\). Find the greatest common factor of all three parts and divide each part by it.
review.sol: Start with the three amounts \(2\tfrac{1}{2}\), \(1\tfrac{1}{4}\), and \(\tfrac{5}{8}\). The smallest denominator that fits all of them is 8, so scaling every part up by 8 will clear the fractions while keeping the ratio the same. Soy wax becomes \(2\tfrac{1}{2}\times 8 = 20\), beeswax becomes \(1\tfrac{1}{4}\times 8 = 10\), and fragrance oil becomes \(\tfrac{5}{8}\times 8 = 5\). That gives the whole-number ratio \(20:10:5\). Now divide out the common factor. The greatest common factor of \(20\), \(10\), and \(5\) is \(5\), so divide each part by \(5\) to get \(4:2:1\). Since the parts \(4\), \(2\), and \(1\) share no factor bigger than \(1\), this is fully reduced. The ratio of soy wax to beeswax to fragrance oil is \(\boxed{4:2:1}\).

review.x: A craftsperson lays a mosaic border made of \(96\) small tiles, mixing gold, copper, and slate in the comparison \(1:2:5\), gold to copper to slate, in that order. Use the parts method to find what one part is worth, then answer just this. How many copper tiles are in the border? Type the single number.
hints: Add the three numbers in the ratio to count the parts. There are \(1+2+5=8\) equal parts hidden in those \(96\) tiles, so first work out how many tiles sit in one part.
One part is \(96\div 8=12\) tiles. Copper is the middle number, so it takes up \(2\) of those parts. Multiply to finish.
review.sol: The ratio \(1:2:5\) splits the border into \(1+2+5=8\) equal parts, and all \(96\) tiles fill those \(8\) parts evenly. One part is worth $$96\div 8=12\text{ tiles}.$$ Copper matches the middle number in the ratio, which is \(2\) parts, so the copper count is \(2\times 12=24\). As a quick check, gold takes \(1\times 12=12\) tiles and slate takes \(5\times 12=60\) tiles, and \(12+24+60=96\), which matches the border. So the number of copper tiles is \(\boxed{24}\).

review.x: A jeweler strings a single \(75\)-bead bracelet so that four kinds of beads always appear in the comparison \(2:4:3:6\), reading jade to amber to onyx to pearl in that order. To split the bracelet, add the parts to see how many equal shares the \(75\) beads break into, find what one share is worth, then scale up to the kind you want. How many pearl beads are on the bracelet? Type the single number.
hints: Add all four parts of \(2:4:3:6\) to find how many equal shares the bracelet splits into, then divide \(75\) by that count to learn what a single share is worth.
One share is \(75 \div 15 = 5\) beads. Pearl carries \(6\) of those shares, so multiply \(5\) by \(6\).
review.sol: The comparison \(2:4:3:6\) tells you how the \(75\) beads divide into equal shares. Add every part to count the shares, \(2+4+3+6 = 15\), so the whole bracelet is built from \(15\) equal shares. Find what one share is worth by splitting the total evenly, \(75 \div 15 = 5\), so each single share is \(5\) beads. Now scale up to the kind the question asks about. Pearl holds \(6\) shares, so the pearl count is \(6 \times 5 = 30\). As a check, jade gives \(2 \times 5 = 10\), amber gives \(4 \times 5 = 20\), and onyx gives \(3 \times 5 = 15\), and \(10 + 20 + 15 + 30 = 75\), which matches the bracelet exactly. So the bracelet carries \(\boxed{30}\) pearl beads.

review.x: A robotics club sorts its members into three roles, builders, coders, and designers, kept in the comparison \(8:5:3\) in that order. Tonight every coder showed up and there are \(35\) coders in the room. Use the coder share to figure out what one part of the comparison is worth, then say how many builders the club has. Type the single number.
hints: The coder share is the middle number, \(5\) parts. If those \(5\) parts equal \(35\) coders, what is one part worth?
Builders are the first number, \(8\) parts. Multiply the value of one part by \(8\).
review.sol: The comparison \(8:5:3\) means builders take \(8\) parts, coders take \(5\) parts, and designers take \(3\) parts, all the same size. The coders are the link here, so start there. There are \(35\) coders sitting in \(5\) parts, so one part is worth \(35 \div 5 = 7\) members. Now rescale to builders. Builders take \(8\) parts, and each part is worth \(7\), so the club has \(8 \times 7 = 56\) builders. As a quick check, designers would be \(3 \times 7 = 21\), and \(56 + 35 + 21 = 112\) members in total, which is \(16\) equal parts of \(7\). The number of builders is \(\boxed{56}\).

review.x: A produce crate is packed in the comparison \(2:9:4\), plums to apples to pears, in that exact order. The crate holds 28 pears. Find what one part is worth from the pears, then use it to answer this. How many apples are in the crate? Type the single number.
hints: The pears sit in the last slot of \(2:9:4\), so they take 4 equal parts. If those 4 parts together make 28 pears, what is one single part worth?
Once you know one part is \(28 \div 4 = 7\) pears, the apples take 9 of those same parts. Multiply \(9 \times 7\).
review.sol: In the comparison \(2:9:4\) the pears are the third number, so they are worth 4 parts. The crate holds 28 pears, and those 28 pears fill 4 equal parts, so one part is \(28 \div 4 = 7\) pieces of fruit. Now look at the apples. They are the middle number, worth 9 parts. Each part is 7, so the apples come to \(9 \times 7 = 63\). That gives \(\boxed{63}\).

review.x: A relay team splits its weekly training distance in the comparison \(6:5:4:3\), run to bike to swim to row, in that order. The team knows the bike leg, which is \(5\) parts of the comparison, comes to \(20\) kilometers. Work out what one part is worth, then use that to find the total. How many kilometers is the whole training distance? Type the single number.
hints: The bike leg is worth \(5\) parts and equals \(20\) kilometers. One part is \(20 \div 5\).
Add the parts \(6+5+4+3\) to see how many parts the whole distance is, then multiply that by what one part is worth.
review.sol: The comparison \(6:5:4:3\) is already in lowest terms, since the only number that divides all four parts is \(1\). The bike leg sits at the \(5\) in that comparison, so it is worth \(5\) parts, and you are told those \(5\) parts equal \(20\) kilometers. Rescale to find one part. One part is \(20 \div 5 = 4\) kilometers. The whole distance covers every part, so add them up, \(6+5+4+3 = 18\) parts. Multiply to get the total, \(18 \times 4 = 72\) kilometers. So the whole training distance is \(\boxed{72}\) kilometers.

review.x: A spice jar blends cumin, paprika, and chili in the ratio \(7:2:1\), cumin to paprika to chili in that order. The jar has 18 grams of paprika. How many grams of spice are in the jar altogether? Type the single number.
hints: Paprika sits at 2 parts in the ratio, and those 2 parts are worth 18 grams. Split that to find what a single part weighs.
Once you know one part, add up the parts. The whole jar is \(7+2+1\) parts, so multiply the value of one part by that total.
review.sol: Paprika is the middle number in \(7:2:1\), so it stands for 2 parts. Those 2 parts weigh 18 grams, so one part is \(18\div 2 = 9\) grams. The whole jar is \(7+2+1 = 10\) parts. Multiply the value of one part by the total number of parts to rescale up to the full jar, \(10\times 9 = 90\) grams. As a check, cumin is \(7\times 9 = 63\) grams, paprika is \(2\times 9 = 18\) grams, and chili is \(1\times 9 = 9\) grams, and \(63+18+9 = 90\). \(\boxed{90}\)

review.x: A clockwork toy has three gears in a row. When the first gear turns the second, their turning ratio is \(5:6\). When the second gear turns the third, their turning ratio is \(4:3\). The second gear is shared by both ratios, but it appears as \(6\) in the first one and as \(4\) in the second one. Rescale so the second-gear numbers agree, line up first to second to third, then compare just the first gear to the third gear. What is the ratio of the first gear to the third gear, written as a:b in lowest terms?
hints: The second gear is the link between both ratios. Make its number the same in each one. It shows up as \(6\) and as \(4\), so find the smallest number both \(6\) and \(4\) divide into, which is \(12\).
Scale \(5:6\) up to \(10:12\) and scale \(4:3\) up to \(12:9\). Now the second gear reads \(12\) in both, so the full chain is \(10:12:9\). Read off only the first and third numbers.
review.sol: The second gear is the shared quantity, so the first job is to make its number match in both ratios. It appears as \(6\) in \(5:6\) and as \(4\) in \(4:3\). The smallest number that both \(6\) and \(4\) divide into is \(12\). Rescale the first ratio by multiplying both parts by \(2\), which gives \(5:6 = 10:12\). Rescale the second ratio by multiplying both parts by \(3\), which gives \(4:3 = 12:9\). Now the second gear reads \(12\) in each, so the three gears line up as \(10:12:9\). The first gear is \(10\) and the third gear is \(9\), so their comparison is \(10:9\). The greatest common factor of \(10\) and \(9\) is \(1\), so it is already in lowest terms. \(\boxed{10:9}\)

review.x: A garden supply shop blends a fertilizer from two separate label comparisons. On the first label, nitrogen to phosphorus is given as \(2:3\). On the second label, phosphorus to potash is given as \(9:8\). Phosphorus is the ingredient both labels share, but it shows up as \(3\) in the first comparison and \(9\) in the second, so the two ratios do not line up yet. Rescale the first ratio so its phosphorus number matches the \(9\) in the second, stitch the two together into a single nitrogen to phosphorus to potash ratio, then read off how nitrogen compares to potash. Write the nitrogen to potash ratio in the form a:b in lowest terms.
hints: Phosphorus is the link. It reads \(3\) in the first ratio and \(9\) in the second, so multiply the whole first ratio by something to turn that \(3\) into a \(9\).
Once the phosphorus parts both equal \(9\), write one combined ratio nitrogen to phosphorus to potash, then ignore the phosphorus part and compare just nitrogen to potash.
review.sol: The two ratios share phosphorus, but they measure it with different numbers, so the first job is to make those numbers agree. In \(2:3\) the phosphorus part is \(3\), and in \(9:8\) the phosphorus part is \(9\). Multiply every part of the first ratio by \(3\) to lift that \(3\) up to \(9\). That turns nitrogen to phosphorus from \(2:3\) into \(6:9\). Now both ratios speak about phosphorus as \(9\), so they snap together into a single chain, nitrogen to phosphorus to potash equals \(6:9:8\). The question only asks how nitrogen compares to potash, so pull out those two parts, \(6:8\). The greatest common factor of \(6\) and \(8\) is \(2\), and dividing both by \(2\) gives \(\boxed{3:4}\).

blocks.x: All through Chapter 7 so far, every ratio you built compared things of the same kind. In 7.1 it was mango to yogurt, both scooped in the same cups. In 7.2 it was red kites to yellow kites, gold tiles to slate tiles, each side counted in the very same units. Because the units matched, they cancelled out, and a ratio like that could shrink all the way down to a bare number with nothing attached, like \(3\) to \(2\) or just \(\tfrac{3}{2}\). Now a different sort of comparison walks in. A snack stand near the market posts a sign that reads 20 dollars for 4 pounds of dried cherries. Dollars and pounds are not the same kind of thing at all. One measures money, the other measures weight, and yet the sign really is lining them up against each other. A comparison of two quantities measured in different units is called a rate, and unlike a same-kind ratio, a rate hangs on to its units, because the units are the whole point of it. The fingerprint of a rate is the small word per. Dollars per pound, pages per day, words per minute, heartbeats per second, each one pins a number from one kind of thing onto exactly one unit of another. Here is the climb ahead. First we squeeze any rate down to its per one value, the unit rate, just by dividing. Then we use that single number to settle which of two deals is the better buy. And finally we meet the most famous rate of all, speed, which is nothing fancier than miles per one hour.

A market stall sells dried cherries with a sign that reads \(20\) dollars for \(4\) pounds. A rate like this gets much easier to picture when you shrink it down to a single pound, the per one amount, because then you know exactly what one pound costs. Since \(4\) pounds cost \(20\) dollars and every pound costs the same, split the \(20\) dollars evenly across the \(4\) pounds by dividing. What is the price per pound, in dollars? Type the single number.
hints: The sign packs four pounds together into one price. To find what just one pound costs, share the \(20\) dollars equally among the \(4\) pounds.
Divide the dollars by the pounds, \(\tfrac{20 \text{ dollars}}{4 \text{ pounds}}\). That single quotient is the price for one pound.
blocks.sol: Four pounds together cost \(20\) dollars, and the cherries all sell at the same rate, so to find the price of one pound I share those dollars equally across the pounds by dividing. That gives \(\tfrac{20 \text{ dollars}}{4 \text{ pounds}} = 5\) dollars per pound. So one pound costs \(\boxed{5}\) dollars.

blocks.x: Rates are not only about money. A reader finishes a 426 page novel in exactly 6 days, and she turns the same number of pages every single day. To describe how fast she reads, you want the amount that rides with one day, the pages that go with a single day. Notice the two quantities here are pages and days, measured in different units, so this is a rate, pages per day, not a price. Spread the pages evenly across the days. How many pages per day did she read? Type the single number.
hints: A unit rate is the amount that goes with one of the second quantity. Here the second quantity is days, so you want the pages that go with one day. That means sharing all the pages equally among the 6 days.
Divide the total pages by the number of days. Compute \(426 \div 6\), and the result is the pages she reads each day.
blocks.sol: A rate compares two quantities in different units, and the unit rate is the amount per one of the second quantity. The second quantity is days, so divide the pages by the days. $$\frac{426 \text{ pages}}{6 \text{ days}} = 71 \text{ pages per day}.$$ You can check it by going the other way, since \(71 \text{ pages per day} \times 6 \text{ days} = 426\) pages, the whole novel. So she read \(\boxed{71}\) pages per day.

blocks.x: Here is the new thing you just met. A rate compares two quantities measured in different units, not the same-kind comparison from 7.1 and 7.2, and the word "per" is the dead giveaway, dollars per pound, pages per day, miles per hour. The unit rate is the special version where the second quantity is squeezed down to exactly one, the amount of the first thing that rides along with a single unit of the second, and you find it by dividing the first quantity by the second. So \(\tfrac{20 \text{ dollars}}{4 \text{ pounds}} = 5\) dollars per one pound, and \(\tfrac{426 \text{ pages}}{6 \text{ days}} = 71\) pages per one day. That is what makes a unit rate so handy, it strips a messy comparison down to one clean number you can hold in your head.

Two bottles of olive oil sit side by side on the shelf. The first holds 15 ounces and costs \(\$6.00\). The second holds 24 ounces and costs \(\$8.40\). The bottles are different sizes and the prices are different, so you cannot tell at a glance which one gives you more oil for your money. The trick is to rewrite each price as a cost for a single ounce, then compare. Divide dollars by ounces for each bottle. The better buy is the one with the lower price per ounce, since you pay less for the very same amount of oil. What is the price per ounce of the better buy, in dollars? Type the single number, for example 0.35.
hints: A unit rate is a cost for one of something. For each bottle, divide the total dollars by the number of ounces to get dollars for one ounce. Do this twice, once per bottle.
First bottle: \(\tfrac{\$6.00}{15 \text{ oz}} = \$0.40\) per ounce. Second bottle: \(\tfrac{\$8.40}{24 \text{ oz}} = \$0.35\) per ounce. The better buy is the smaller of these two numbers.
blocks.sol: Find the price per ounce of each bottle by dividing dollars by ounces. First bottle: $$\frac{\$6.00}{15 \text{ oz}} = \$0.40 \text{ per ounce}.$$ Second bottle: $$\frac{\$8.40}{24 \text{ oz}} = \$0.35 \text{ per ounce}.$$ Now compare the two unit rates. The second bottle costs \(\$0.35\) for each ounce while the first costs \(\$0.40\) for each ounce, so the second bottle is the better buy because you pay less for the same amount of oil. The price per ounce of the better buy is \(\boxed{0.35}\) dollars.

blocks.x: When two deals come in different sizes at different prices, you cannot rank them just by glancing, since a bigger price might only mean a bigger package. So shrink both down to the same footing and ask what one single unit costs, one ounce or one pound or one anything, by dividing the price by how many units you get. Once both are written as a price per one, the comparison is instant, and the better buy is simply whichever has the lower price per unit. That same per-one move ranks anything measured this way, fewer dollars per ounce, more miles per gallon, more words per minute, and it is exactly the trick we are about to turn loose on speed.

<svg viewBox="0 0 440 318" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A unit-rate comparison of two olive-oil bottles, each rewritten as a price for one ounce. The left card is bottle A, fifteen ounces for six dollars, with the division six dollars divided by fifteen ounces equals zero point four zero dollars per ounce. The right card is bottle B, twenty four ounces for eight dollars and forty cents, with the division eight dollars forty divided by twenty four ounces equals zero point three five dollars per ounce. Below the cards a short number line is labeled price for ONE ounce. The value zero point three five from bottle B sits to the left and lower in price, and zero point four zero from bottle A sits to its right. The right card, bottle B, is highlighted in gold and tagged BETTER BUY, with a note that the lower price for a single ounce wins even though bottle B costs more in total.">

blocks.cap: Writing each option as a price for one ounce makes the cheaper deal jump out. Bottle A works out to \(\tfrac{\$6.00}{15 \text{ oz}} = 0.40\) dollars per ounce, while bottle B is \(\tfrac{\$8.40}{24 \text{ oz}} = 0.35\) dollars per ounce. On the shared baseline \(0.35\) sits below \(0.40\), so even though bottle B costs more in total, its lower price per unit makes it the better buy.

blocks.x: Now meet the most important rate of all. A long coastal highway drive covers 195 miles in 3 hours of steady cruising, no stops. Speed is just a rate of distance to time, and its units, miles per hour, literally tell you the operation, miles divided by hours. The "per one" here is per one hour, the number of miles the car covers in a single hour. Divide the distance by the time. What is the speed in miles per hour? Type the single number.
hints: The unit miles per hour is a recipe. It says take the miles and divide by the hours, which lands you on the miles covered in one hour.
Compute \(195 \div 3\). That tells you how many miles fit into each single hour of driving.
blocks.sol: Speed is distance divided by time, and the unit miles per hour spells out exactly that division. Put the miles on top and the hours on the bottom, then divide. $$\frac{195 \text{ miles}}{3 \text{ hours}} = 65 \text{ miles per hour}$$ So in every single hour the car covers 65 miles, which is the meaning of the "per one hour". The speed is \(\boxed{65}\) miles per hour.

blocks.x: A delivery van settles into a steady cruise of \(48\) miles per hour and holds that exact pace on the interstate for \(7\) hours straight. This time the rate and the time are the things you know, and the distance is the mystery. Flip the speed relationship around. Saying \(48\) miles per hour means \(48\) fresh miles slide under the wheels in every single hour, so \(7\) hours stack up \(7\) helpings of those miles. How many miles did the van travel?
hints: Speed tells you the miles gained in one hour. To get the miles for all the hours, you do not divide here, you build up, so distance is speed times time.
Multiply the \(48\) miles in each hour by the \(7\) hours the van kept driving.
blocks.sol: Speed is distance per hour, so to recover the distance you multiply the speed by the number of hours. That gives \(\text{distance} = \text{speed} \times \text{time}\). Plug in the steady pace and the driving time, \(48 \tfrac{\text{miles}}{\text{hour}} \times 7 \text{ hours} = 336 \text{ miles}\). The "hours" in the rate cancels the "hours" of driving, leaving plain miles, which is exactly the unit the question wants. The van covered \(\boxed{336}\) miles.

blocks.x: Speed is just a rate, the rate of distance to time, and you find it by dividing the distance by the time. That little unit label is really an instruction in disguise, the thing before the word "per" is what you measured and the thing after it is what you divide by, so "miles per hour" means exactly \(\tfrac{\text{miles}}{\text{hours}}\) and the word "per" is the division bar said out loud. Because a speed in miles per hour is already a per one hour amount, two movers line up cleanly once both are written that way, and the faster one is simply whoever covers more distance in a single hour. It is the same per one trick you used to crown the better deal, only now it is distance over time instead of dollars over ounces.

A long endurance route stretches 392 miles across the countryside, and a cyclist on a fast e-bike can hold a steady 56 miles per hour the whole way. This time you already know the distance and the speed, and the thing you want is the time. Think about what speed really tells you. At 56 miles per hour, every hour swallows up another 56 miles of road. So the question becomes how many of those 56 mile hours fit inside 392 miles, and that is exactly distance divided by speed. How many hours does the whole ride take?
hints: Speed connects distance, time, and miles per hour. You have the distance and the speed, so flip the relationship around. Time is distance divided by speed.
Each hour covers 56 miles, so count how many 56 mile chunks fit in 392 miles. Compute \(392 \div 56\).
blocks.sol: Speed says distance divided by time, so to get the time you rearrange it the other way and use time equals distance divided by speed. Drop in the numbers, \(\tfrac{392 \text{ miles}}{56 \text{ miles per hour}} = 7 \text{ hours}\). You can check it by going forward, since \(56 \text{ miles per hour} \times 7 \text{ hours} = 392 \text{ miles}\), which matches the route exactly. The ride takes \(\boxed{7}\) hours.

blocks.x: Speed, distance, and time are three names for one fact, and that fact will rearrange itself to give you whichever name is missing. Starting from speed equals distance divided by time, you can find a distance by multiplying instead, \(\text{distance} = \text{speed} \times \text{time}\), and you can find a time by dividing the other way, \(\text{time} = \tfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}}\). A handy trick is to cover up the quantity you are missing and look at the other two, if the missing one usually sits on top you multiply the two below it, and if it sits under the line you divide. So as soon as you know any two of the three, the last one drops right out.

<svg viewBox="0 0 440 320" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A speed relationship figure built around one triangle that holds three quantities. The top half of the triangle holds distance, and the bottom half is split into two corners, speed on the left and time on the right, with a horizontal line separating top from bottom. The layout means cover one corner to read the operation. Beside the triangle the same fact is written three ways. Speed equals distance divided by time, with the worked tag speed equals 195 over 3 equals 65 miles per hour. Distance equals speed times time, with the worked tag distance equals 48 times 7 equals 336 miles. Time equals distance divided by speed, with the worked tag time equals 392 over 56 equals 7 hours. A note explains that a quantity sitting on top means multiply the two below it, and a quantity under the line means divide.">
  <text x="220" y="18" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="var(--mid)">ONE TRIANGLE, ONE FACT, THREE WAYS</text>

  <text x="100" y="247" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="8" fill="var(--slate)">on top of the line,</text>

blocks.cap: All three forms are the same fact, \(s=\tfrac{d}{t}\), seen from three sides. Whatever quantity sits on top means multiply the two below it, and a quantity under the line means divide, so \(s=\tfrac{195}{3}=65\) mph, \(d=48\times 7=336\) miles, and \(t=\tfrac{392}{56}=7\) hours. The units tell you which one you need, and the triangle tells you whether to multiply or divide.

blocks.x: An electric scooter glides across town and covers \(21\) miles in \(45\) minutes. You want its speed, but here is the snag. A speed in miles per hour needs the time measured in hours, and the time you were handed is in minutes. Stay inside the single dimension of time and fix that first. Forty five minutes is \(45\) out of the \(60\) minutes in an hour, which is \(\tfrac{45}{60} = \tfrac{3}{4}\) of an hour, or \(0.75\) hours. Now you have miles and you have hours, so the speed is the miles divided by that time. What is the scooter's speed in miles per hour? Type the single number.
hints: The unit you want is miles per hour, so the time underneath the miles has to be in hours, not minutes. Turn 45 minutes into a fraction of an hour first by writing it over the 60 minutes in one hour.
You have \(\tfrac{45}{60} = \tfrac{3}{4}\) of an hour, which is \(0.75\) hours. Now divide the distance by that time, \(21 \div 0.75\), and the result comes out in miles per hour.
blocks.sol: First handle the time, staying inside the one dimension of time. Forty five minutes out of the \(60\) minutes in an hour is \(\tfrac{45}{60} = \tfrac{3}{4}\) of an hour, which is \(0.75\) hours. Now the units line up, miles on top and hours on the bottom, so divide. $$\frac{21 \text{ miles}}{0.75 \text{ hours}} = 28 \text{ miles per hour}$$ Dividing by \(0.75\) is the same as dividing by \(\tfrac{3}{4}\), and \(21 \div \tfrac{3}{4} = 21 \times \tfrac{4}{3} = 28\), so the units check out and the speed is \(\boxed{28}\) miles per hour.

blocks.x: A commuter train pulls out of the station and settles into a steady \(64\) miles per hour, holding that pace for \(105\) minutes of open track. You want to know how far it traveled, and you already know that distance equals speed times time. But there is a snag. The speed counts miles for every one hour, while the time is handed to you in minutes, so the two units do not line up yet. Before you multiply, the time needs to be expressed in hours so it matches the speed. Once you have done that, multiply the speed by the time in hours. How many miles did the train travel?
hints: The speed is per hour, so the time must be in hours too. Turn \(105\) minutes into hours by dividing by \(60\), since one hour is \(60\) minutes. You should get \(\tfrac{105}{60} = 1.75\) hours.
Now that the units match, use distance \(=\) speed \(\times\) time. Multiply \(64\) miles per hour by \(1.75\) hours and watch the "hours" cancel, leaving miles.
blocks.sol: First make the units agree. The speed is in miles per hour, so the time has to be in hours. Since one hour is \(60\) minutes, divide the minutes by \(60\). $$105 \text{ minutes} = \tfrac{105}{60} \text{ hours} = 1.75 \text{ hours}$$ Now the units match, so apply distance \(=\) speed \(\times\) time. $$64 \tfrac{\text{miles}}{\text{hour}} \times 1.75 \text{ hours} = 112 \text{ miles}$$ The "hours" on top and bottom cancel, leaving miles, which is exactly the unit a distance should have. The train traveled \(\boxed{112}\) miles.

blocks.x: The units of a rate are quietly bossing you around, telling you exactly what to feed it. A speed in miles per hour wants its time measured in hours, so when a time shows up wearing minutes you have to turn it into hours first, by dividing the minutes by \(60\). That is how \(90\) minutes becomes \(\tfrac{90}{60}=1.5\) hours, \(45\) minutes becomes \(0.75\) of an hour, and \(40\) minutes becomes \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of an hour. Keep the fix inside one kind of measurement, here time turning minutes into hours, and once the time unit matches the rate the answer falls out in exactly the unit you wanted.

A weekend road trip happens in two stretches. The first stretch is slow city driving, 84 miles in 2 hours of stop and go traffic. The second stretch opens up onto the highway, 161 miles in 3 hours of smooth cruising. It is tempting to find each stretch's speed and average those two numbers together, but that is not what average speed means. Average speed asks one honest question about the whole trip, how many miles did you cover for each single hour you were driving. So gather every mile and every hour, then split the total distance evenly across the total time. What is the average speed for the whole trip, in miles per hour? Type the single number.
hints: Average speed is not the average of the two separate speeds. It is the total distance you traveled divided by the total time you spent. First add up all the miles, then add up all the hours.
The miles add to \(84 + 161 = 245\) miles and the hours add to \(2 + 3 = 5\) hours. Now divide the total distance by the total time, \(245 \div 5\).
blocks.sol: Average speed is the total distance covered divided by the total time spent, not the mean of the two leg speeds. First gather the totals. The whole distance is $$84 \text{ miles} + 161 \text{ miles} = 245 \text{ miles}.$$ The whole time is $$2 \text{ hours} + 3 \text{ hours} = 5 \text{ hours}.$$ Now divide the total distance by the total time. $$\frac{245 \text{ miles}}{5 \text{ hours}} = 49 \text{ miles per hour}.$$ Notice why averaging the two speeds would mislead you. The city leg ran at \(\tfrac{84 \text{ miles}}{2 \text{ hours}} = 42\) miles per hour and the highway leg ran at \(\tfrac{161 \text{ miles}}{3 \text{ hours}} \approx 53.7\) miles per hour. The honest trip average sits at \(\boxed{49}\) miles per hour, found from the totals.

blocks.x: Here is the big idea. Average speed for a whole trip is the total distance divided by the total time, the one steady speed that would carry you the same way over that same total time. It is almost never just the average of the leg speeds, because you spend more time at the slower speed, and that extra time pulls the average down toward the slow part. So gather the entire trip first, add up every mile and add up every hour, and only then divide. Total over total, not the middle of the two speeds.

A courier rides out to a quiet farm and then back home along the very same road, so the two legs cover an equal distance. On the way out she fights a stiff headwind and manages only \(30\) miles per hour. On the way back that same wind is behind her, so she flies along at \(70\) miles per hour. It is very tempting to call the average speed the number halfway between, \(50\) miles per hour, but that is the classic trap, and the reason is that she spends more time crawling at the slow speed than she does at the fast one. To keep the arithmetic clean, take the one-way distance to be \(210\) miles. Find the time for the trip out, find the time for the trip back, add them to get the total time, add the two equal distances to get the total distance, and then divide total distance by total time. What is the average speed for the whole round trip, in miles per hour?
hints: Average speed is never the average of the two speeds. It is total distance divided by total time, so your first job is to find each leg's time. For the trip out, time is distance divided by speed, which is \(\tfrac{210 \text{ miles}}{30 \text{ miles per hour}}\). Do the same for the trip back at \(70\) miles per hour.

The trip out takes \(\tfrac{210}{30} = 7\) hours and the trip back takes \(\tfrac{210}{70} = 3\) hours, so the total time is \(10\) hours. The total distance is the two equal legs added together, \(210 + 210 = 420\) miles. Now divide \(420\) miles by \(10\) hours.
blocks.sol: Average speed is total distance divided by total time, not the mean of the two speeds, so start by finding how long each leg takes. Time is distance divided by speed. The trip out covers \(210\) miles at \(30\) miles per hour, which takes $$\frac{210 \text{ miles}}{30 \text{ miles per hour}} = 7 \text{ hours}.$$ The trip back covers the same \(210\) miles at \(70\) miles per hour, which takes $$\frac{210 \text{ miles}}{70 \text{ miles per hour}} = 3 \text{ hours}.$$ Add the times to get the total time, \(7 + 3 = 10\) hours. Add the two equal legs to get the total distance, \(210 + 210 = 420\) miles. Now divide total distance by total time, $$\frac{420 \text{ miles}}{10 \text{ hours}} = 42 \text{ miles per hour}.$$ Notice this lands below \(50\), pulled toward the slow speed because she spent \(7\) of her \(10\) hours crawling into the wind. The average speed for the round trip is \(\boxed{42}\) miles per hour.

A horizontal trip bar is split into two segments sized by time. The left segment, two fifths of the bar, is leg one, labeled 84 miles in 2 hours, with the leg speed 42 miles per hour noted small. The right segment, three fifths of the bar, is leg two, labeled 161 miles in 3 hours, with the leg speed about 53.7 miles per hour noted small. Beneath the bar the correct computation is shown prominently. Total distance 84 plus 161 equals 245 miles, total time 2 plus 3 equals 5 hours, and average speed 245 divided by 5 equals 49 miles per hour, with the 49 highlighted in gold. blocks.cap: Average speed on a two-leg trip is total distance over total time, not the average of the two leg speeds. Here the trip covers \(84+161=245\) miles in \(2+3=5\) hours, so the average speed is \(\tfrac{245 \text{ miles}}{5 \text{ hours}} = 49\) miles per hour. The plain mean of the leg speeds, \(\tfrac{42+53.7}{2}\approx 47.8\), misses it, because the trip spends more time on the faster leg, which tugs the true average up above that simple midpoint.

blocks.x: Two office printers are tearing through a giant stack of reports. Printer A spits out 1,800 pages in 40 minutes. Printer B spits out 1,450 pages in 25 minutes. They were switched on for different lengths of time, so the raw page totals do not tell you which machine is actually quicker. This is the same per one move you used on the grocery deals and the speeds. Bring each printer down to its rate for a single minute, pages divided by minutes, and the faster one will have more pages every minute. What is the rate of the faster printer, in pages per minute?
hints: The page totals are over different times, so you cannot compare them head to head yet. Knock each printer down to one minute first. For each printer divide its pages by its minutes.
blocks.sol: Bring each printer to its per one minute rate by dividing pages by minutes. For Printer A, \(\tfrac{1{,}800 \text{ pages}}{40 \text{ minutes}} = 45\) pages per minute. For Printer B, \(\tfrac{1{,}450 \text{ pages}}{25 \text{ minutes}} = 58\) pages per minute. Now they are on equal footing, both measured over a single minute. Since 58 pages per minute is more than 45 pages per minute, Printer B is the faster machine, churning out \(\boxed{58}\) pages every minute.

blocks.x: Here is where the word "knot" for ship speed comes from. Centuries ago a sailor measured how fast his ship was moving with a chip log, a wedge of wood tied to a long rope and tossed off the back of the ship so it dragged in the water and stayed put while the ship pulled away. The rope had knots tied into it at even spacing along its length, and one sailor let it run freely over the side while another flipped a small sand glass to time a fixed short interval. When the sand ran out he pinched the rope and counted how many knots had slipped through his fingers, and that count was the ship's speed. That is why speed at sea is still given in knots today, where one knot means one nautical mile per hour. So a knot is itself a rate, a distance per one hour, the very same "per one" idea running through everything in this lesson.

Two service trucks sit on a long straight highway with \(300\) miles of empty road between them. At the same instant they pull out and drive straight toward each other to meet for a parts handoff. One truck rolls along at \(45\) miles per hour, the other at \(55\) miles per hour. Here is the trick to see. In a single hour the first truck eats \(45\) miles of the gap and the second eats \(55\) more, so together they shrink the gap by \(45 + 55\) miles every hour. That total is the speed at which the gap itself closes, and the meeting time is just the starting gap divided by that closing speed. How many hours until the two trucks meet? Type the single number of hours.
hints: You do not have to track each truck on its own. Think about the gap between them. Every hour it gets smaller by both speeds added together, so the gap closes at \(45 + 55\) miles per hour.
Once you know how fast the gap closes, treat it like any time question. Time equals the distance to close divided by the closing speed, so divide the \(300\) mile gap by that combined speed.
blocks.sol: Each hour the two trucks together remove both of their speeds from the gap, so the gap closes at the combined speed \(45 + 55 = 100\) miles per hour. The meeting time is the starting gap divided by that closing speed, \(\tfrac{300 \text{ miles}}{100 \text{ miles per hour}} = 3 \text{ hours}\). You can check it. In \(3\) hours the slower truck covers \(45 \times 3 = 135\) miles and the faster truck covers \(55 \times 3 = 165\) miles, and \(135 + 165 = 300\) miles, exactly the road between them. So they meet after \(\boxed{3}\) hours.

blocks.x: Here is the move that makes two movers easy. When two things head straight toward each other, you do not have to follow each one separately, you just watch the gap between them, because every hour that gap loses both distances at once, one bite taken from each end. So the gap shrinks at the combined speed, the two speeds added together, exactly as if one mover were covering the whole gap alone at that faster pace. That means the time until they meet is just the starting gap divided by the combined speed, plain time equals distance over speed with the combined speed doing the work.

review.x: A roadside fruit cart pins up a sign that reads \(7\) pounds of crisp green apples for \(28\) dollars, and every pound costs the same. A shopper wants to know the cost of just a single pound so she can compare it to the orchard down the road. To turn a price for many pounds into a price for one pound, you divide the dollars by the pounds. What is the price per pound, in dollars? Type the single number.
hints: A unit rate is the cost of exactly one of something. Here you want dollars for one pound, so split the total price evenly across all the pounds.
Divide the dollars by the pounds, \(28 \div 7\). The result is how many dollars one single pound costs.
review.sol: A unit rate tells you the cost of one of the second quantity, here one pound. The sign gives a price for many pounds at once, and since every pound costs the same you can share the total evenly. Divide the dollars by the pounds, \(\tfrac{28 \text{ dollars}}{7 \text{ pounds}} = 4\) dollars per pound. You can check this by going backward, since \(4 \text{ dollars per pound} \times 7 \text{ pounds} = 28\) dollars, which matches the sign. So one pound costs \(\boxed{4}\) dollars.

review.x: A resting heart beats \(312\) times over \(4\) minutes at a steady pace. Express this as a unit rate, beats per one minute, by dividing the beats by the minutes. How many beats per minute is that? Type the single number.
hints: A unit rate tells you the amount for ONE minute, so you want beats divided by minutes.
Compute \(312 \div 4\). That single number is your beats per minute.
review.sol: A unit rate is the amount per one of the second quantity, here per one minute, so I divide the total beats by the total minutes. $$\frac{312 \text{ beats}}{4 \text{ minutes}} = 78 \text{ beats per minute}$$ So the heart beats at \(\boxed{78}\) beats per minute.

review.x: Two bags of the same brown rice sit side by side on a market shelf. The smaller bag holds 12 ounces and costs 3 dollars and 60 cents. The larger bag holds 20 ounces and costs 5 dollars even. Find the price for a single ounce of each bag, then pick the better buy, which is the one with the lower price per ounce. What is the price per ounce of the better buy, in dollars?
hints: A unit rate is the price for one ounce, so divide the cost of each bag by its number of ounces. The cheaper bag wins.
Smaller bag is \(3.60 \div 12\) and larger bag is \(5.00 \div 20\). Compare the two per-ounce prices and report the smaller one.
review.sol: Turn each bag into a price for one ounce by dividing the cost by the number of ounces. The smaller bag gives \(\tfrac{\$3.60}{12 \text{ ounces}} = \$0.30\) per ounce. The larger bag gives \(\tfrac{\$5.00}{20 \text{ ounces}} = \$0.25\) per ounce. Now compare. Since \(0.25\) is less than \(0.30\), the larger bag is the better buy, and its price per ounce is \(\boxed{0.25}\) dollars.

review.x: A small workshop runs a batch on two assembly machines. Machine A turns out 240 parts in 8 hours, while machine B turns out 350 parts in 10 hours. Write each one as a unit rate in parts per one hour, then decide which machine is faster, meaning it makes more parts each hour. What is the rate of the faster machine, in parts per hour?
hints: A unit rate is parts per one hour, so divide each machine's parts by its number of hours. That gives you two numbers you can line up and compare.
Machine A gives \(\tfrac{240}{8}\) and machine B gives \(\tfrac{350}{10}\). Work out both, then pick the larger one as your answer.
review.sol: Find the unit rate for each machine by dividing its parts by its hours. For machine A, \(\tfrac{240 \text{ parts}}{8 \text{ hours}} = 30\) parts per hour. For machine B, \(\tfrac{350 \text{ parts}}{10 \text{ hours}} = 35\) parts per hour. Now compare the two per-hour numbers. Machine B makes 35 parts every hour and machine A makes only 30, so machine B is the faster one. Its rate is \(\boxed{35}\) parts per hour.

review.x: A regional commuter train glides along its route, covering \(280\) miles in \(5\) hours while holding a steady pace the whole way. To find how fast it travels, divide the distance by the time. What is the train's speed in miles per hour? Type the single number.
hints: Speed is distance divided by time. Set up the rate as \(\tfrac{280 \text{ miles}}{5 \text{ hours}}\), which tells you how many miles the train covers in one hour.
Divide \(280\) by \(5\). Think about what number times \(5\) lands on \(280\).
review.sol: Speed is a rate, the distance covered for each hour of travel. Divide the total distance by the total time. $$\frac{280 \text{ miles}}{5 \text{ hours}} = 56 \text{ miles per hour}$$ A quick check, \(56 \times 5 = 280\) miles, which matches the distance given. So the train's speed is \(\boxed{56}\) miles per hour.

review.x: A maglev shuttle on a test track holds a perfectly steady \(62\) miles per hour for \(4\) hours straight. Using the idea that distance equals speed times time, how many miles does the shuttle travel?
hints: Distance equals speed times time. The speed is \(62\) miles per hour and the time is \(4\) hours.
Multiply \(62\) by \(4\). Each hour adds another \(62\) miles to the total.
review.sol: Speed stays steady, so distance equals speed times time. The speed is \(62\) miles per hour and the time is \(4\) hours, so the miles per hour multiply by the number of hours and the hours cancel out, leaving miles. $$62 \text{ miles per hour} \times 4 \text{ hours} = 248 \text{ miles}$$ The shuttle covers \(\boxed{248}\) miles.

review.x: A tour bus has a long stretch of highway ahead, 414 miles in all, and it holds a steady cruising speed of 46 miles per hour the whole way. Since the distance and the speed are known, the time is the distance divided by the speed. How many hours does the trip take?
hints: When you know how far you are going and how fast, time is distance divided by speed. Set up \(\tfrac{414 \text{ miles}}{46 \text{ miles per hour}}\).
Divide 414 by 46. Think about what number times 46 lands on 414.
review.sol: Speed is distance divided by time, so rearranged it gives time equals distance divided by speed. The bus covers 414 miles at 46 miles per hour, so $$\text{time} = \frac{414 \text{ miles}}{46 \text{ miles per hour}} = 9 \text{ hours}.$$ The miles cancel against the miles in "miles per hour" and leave hours, which is exactly the unit we want. So the trip takes \(\boxed{9}\) hours.

review.x: A cyclist on a time trial covers 35 miles in 50 minutes. To get a speed in miles per hour you need the time in hours, and 50 minutes is \(\tfrac{50}{60}\) of an hour, which is \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) of an hour. Once the time is in hours, divide the distance by the time. What is the speed in miles per hour? Type the single number.
hints: Speed in miles per hour means miles divided by hours, so first get the time into hours. Fifty minutes out of sixty is \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) of an hour.
Now compute \(35 \div \tfrac{5}{6}\). Dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal, so \(35 \times \tfrac{6}{5}\).
review.sol: First put the time into hours. Fifty minutes is \(\tfrac{50}{60} = \tfrac{5}{6}\) of an hour. Speed is distance divided by time, so $$\frac{35 \text{ miles}}{\tfrac{5}{6} \text{ hour}} = 35 \times \frac{6}{5} = \frac{210}{5} = 42 \text{ miles per hour}.$$ The speed is \(\boxed{42}\).

review.x: A survey drone holds a steady speed of \(72\) miles per hour while it maps a coastline. The flight lasts \(25\) minutes. How many miles does the drone cover during that flight?
hints: Speed is in miles per hour, so the time has to be in hours too. Write \(25\) minutes as a fraction of an hour, since one hour is \(60\) minutes.
\(25\) minutes is \(\tfrac{25}{60}=\tfrac{5}{12}\) of an hour. Now multiply the speed by that fraction of an hour to get the distance.
review.sol: The speed is given per hour, so first change \(25\) minutes into hours. One hour is \(60\) minutes, so \(25\) minutes is \(\tfrac{25}{60}=\tfrac{5}{12}\) of an hour. Distance is speed times time, so \(72 \text{ miles per hour} \times \tfrac{5}{12} \text{ hour} = \tfrac{72 \times 5}{12} = \tfrac{360}{12} = 30\) miles. The drone covers \(\boxed{30}\) miles.

review.x: A delivery scooter cruises at a steady \(36\) miles per hour and has a package to drop off \(9\) miles away. Riding at that constant speed the whole way, how many minutes does the trip take?
hints: Time equals distance divided by speed, so the time in hours is \(9 \div 36\). That gives you a fraction of an hour.
An hour is \(60\) minutes, so multiply your fraction of an hour by \(60\) to turn it into minutes.
review.sol: Start with time equals distance divided by speed. The distance is \(9\) miles and the speed is \(36\) miles per hour, so the time in hours is $$\frac{9 \text{ miles}}{36 \text{ miles per hour}} = \tfrac{1}{4} \text{ hour}.$$ Now turn that quarter of an hour into minutes. One hour is \(60\) minutes, so multiply. $$\tfrac{1}{4} \text{ hour} \times 60 \text{ minutes per hour} = 15 \text{ minutes}.$$ The trip takes \(\boxed{15}\) minutes.

review.x: A delivery van runs a route in two legs. The first leg covers 90 miles in 2 hours through the hills, then the second leg covers 140 miles in 3 hours along the flat highway. Remember that average speed is the total distance divided by the total time, not the mean of the two leg speeds. What is the average speed for the whole route in miles per hour?
hints: Add up everything first. The total distance is \(90 + 140\) miles, and the total time is \(2 + 3\) hours.
Average speed is total distance over total time, so divide \(230\) miles by \(5\) hours. Do not average \(45\) and \(46\tfrac{2}{3}\).
review.sol: First gather the whole trip. The total distance is \(90 \text{ miles} + 140 \text{ miles} = 230 \text{ miles}\), and the total time is \(2 \text{ hours} + 3 \text{ hours} = 5 \text{ hours}\). Average speed is the total distance divided by the total time, so $$\frac{230 \text{ miles}}{5 \text{ hours}} = 46 \text{ miles per hour}.$$ Notice this is not the mean of the two leg speeds, which would be \(\tfrac{45 + 46\tfrac{2}{3}}{2} = 45\tfrac{5}{6}\), because the van spent different amounts of time on each leg. The average speed for the whole route is \(\boxed{46}\) miles per hour.

review.x: A kayaker paddles up a calm river channel, then turns around and paddles back down the very same stretch, so the two legs cover equal distance. Fighting the current upstream, she moves at \(20\) miles per hour. Riding with the current downstream, she moves at \(30\) miles per hour. It is tempting to call the round trip average \(25\), the simple midpoint of the two speeds, but the two legs take different amounts of time, so that is not right. To get clean numbers, take the one-way distance to be \(60\) miles. Find the time for each leg, add them, then divide the total distance by the total time. What is the average speed for the whole round trip, in miles per hour?
hints: Average speed is never the mean of the two speeds. It is the total distance for the trip divided by the total time the trip took, so you need both totals first. Find how long each leg lasts using time = distance divided by speed.

Upstream covers \(60\) miles at \(20\) miles per hour, which is \(\tfrac{60}{20} = 3\) hours. Downstream covers \(60\) miles at \(30\) miles per hour, which is \(\tfrac{60}{30} = 2\) hours. The whole trip is \(120\) miles in total. Now divide that total distance by the total time.
review.sol: Average speed is total distance over total time, not the average of the two speeds, because the slower leg eats up more of the clock. Handle each leg with time = distance divided by speed. Upstream takes \(\tfrac{60 \text{ miles}}{20 \text{ miles per hour}} = 3\) hours. Downstream takes \(\tfrac{60 \text{ miles}}{30 \text{ miles per hour}} = 2\) hours. The round trip covers \(60 + 60 = 120\) miles in \(3 + 2 = 5\) hours. So the average speed is \(\tfrac{120 \text{ miles}}{5 \text{ hours}} = 24\) miles per hour. Notice it landed below the midpoint of \(25\), pulled down because the slow upstream leg lasted longer. \(\boxed{24}\)

review.x: Two delivery drones lift off at the exact same moment from opposite ends of a straight maintenance tunnel that is \(264\) miles long, and they fly toward each other down the middle of it. The first drone holds a steady \(38\) miles per hour, and the second holds a steady \(50\) miles per hour. Because they are flying straight at each other, the empty space between them shrinks every hour by the two speeds added together. How many hours go by before the two drones meet? Type the single number.
hints: When two movers head straight toward each other, the gap between them closes at the two speeds combined. Add \(38\) and \(50\) to find how many miles of gap vanish each hour.
Once you know the combined closing speed in miles per hour, divide the starting gap of \(264\) miles by that speed to find the time. Time equals distance divided by speed.
review.sol: When two movers travel straight toward each other, you do not have to track them one at a time. The space between them is what matters, and that space disappears at the two speeds working together. So first add the speeds to get the combined closing speed, \(38 + 50 = 88\) miles per hour. That means the gap shrinks by \(88\) miles every hour. The gap starts at \(264\) miles, and time is distance divided by speed, so divide the starting gap by the closing speed, \(\tfrac{264 \text{ miles}}{88 \text{ miles per hour}} = 3\) hours. You can check it. In \(3\) hours the first drone covers \(38 \times 3 = 114\) miles and the second covers \(50 \times 3 = 150\) miles, and \(114 + 150 = 264\) miles, exactly the length of the tunnel, so they meet right as the gap closes. The drones meet after \(\boxed{3}\) hours.

review.x: A long straight rail-trail has two cyclists who start at opposite ends, 350 miles apart, and pedal straight toward each other. One holds a steady 30 miles per hour and the other a steady 40 miles per hour. Each hour the gap between them shrinks by their combined speed. First work out the moment they meet, then figure out the distance the faster cyclist has covered by then. How many miles has the faster cyclist traveled when they meet?
hints: The gap closes at the combined speed, so add the two speeds. Time to meet is the 350 mile gap divided by that combined speed.
Once you know the meeting time in hours, use distance equals speed times time for the faster cyclist, the one going 40 miles per hour.
review.sol: The two cyclists approach each other, so the distance between them drops at the combined speed. Add the speeds to get \(30 + 40 = 70\) miles per hour. The 350 mile gap closes at that rate, so the time until they meet is \(\tfrac{350 \text{ miles}}{70 \text{ miles per hour}} = 5\) hours. Now use distance equals speed times time for the faster cyclist, who rides at 40 miles per hour. In 5 hours that cyclist covers \(40 \times 5 = 200\) miles. As a check, the slower cyclist covers \(30 \times 5 = 150\) miles, and \(200 + 150 = 350\) miles, the full gap, so the meeting works out. The faster cyclist has traveled \(\boxed{200}\) miles.

blocks.x: All through the last three lessons you kept doing the same quiet move without ever giving it a name. Back in 7.1 you climbed a ladder of equivalent ratios, turning \(3:4\) into \(6:8\) by multiplying both parts of a ratio by the same number. In 7.3 you found a unit rate and scaled it up to whatever amount you needed. Every single time, you were taking one ratio and insisting that a bigger or smaller ratio was secretly the same. It is time to name what was really happening. When you write one ratio on the left, an equal sign in the middle, and a matching ratio on the right, you have a proportion, an equation that claims two ratios are the same size. It looks like \(\frac{a}{b} = \frac{c}{d}\), two fractions staring across an equals sign. Two quantities are proportional when their ratio stays steady no matter how big or how small they grow. Double one side and the other side doubles to keep the ratio constant, and that steadiness is the whole reason a proportion is allowed to exist. Now for the promise. Every earlier lesson tiptoed around one famous shortcut for cracking these open, and this is the lesson where it finally walks in the door, cross-multiplication. It is not some strange new way of thinking. Once a proportion is set up, it is just an equation, the same kind of thing you cleared denominators on back in 6.2, so you already know how to finish the job. Here is the path ahead. First you will see when a relationship actually deserves a proportion at all, then you will learn two honest ways to solve one, the quick scaling move you already half know and the dependable new shortcut that works every time.

A stone fountain in a courtyard pours water at a steady, unchanging rate. You time it for \(2\) minutes and catch \(18\) liters in a tank below. A friend who stays longer reports that after \(5\) minutes the fountain had released \(45\) liters. First check whether the flow is really steady. In the first stretch the rate is \(\frac{18\text{ liters}}{2\text{ min}} = 9\) liters every minute, and over the longer stretch it is \(\frac{45\text{ liters}}{5\text{ min}} = 9\) liters every minute too. The ratio of liters to minutes never drifts, so the relationship is proportional. Keeping that exact same flow, how many liters pour out in \(8\) minutes?
hints: Because the flow is proportional, the ratio of minutes to liters stays the same at every moment. Write two equal ratios with minutes on top and liters on the bottom in both, using \(x\) for the unknown liters. One side is the \(2\) minute reading, the other is the \(8\) minute pour. That gives you \(\frac{2}{18} = \frac{8}{x}\).

To solve a proportion, cross-multiply. For \(\frac{a}{b} = \frac{c}{d}\) the cross products are equal, so \(a\cdot d = b\cdot c\). Apply that to \(\frac{2}{18} = \frac{8}{x}\) to clear the denominators, then solve the simple equation for \(x\).
blocks.sol: Set up a proportion with matching units in matching positions, minutes over liters on both sides, and let \(x\) be the liters in \(8\) minutes. $$\frac{2}{18} = \frac{8}{x}$$ Cross-multiply, which is just clearing both denominators at once. For \(\frac{a}{b} = \frac{c}{d}\) the rule \(a\cdot d = b\cdot c\) holds, so $$2\cdot x = 18\cdot 8$$ $$2x = 144$$ Divide both sides by \(2\). $$x = \frac{144}{2} = 72$$ A quick sanity check, the steady rate is \(9\) liters per minute, and \(9\times 8 = 72\), which matches. So in \(8\) minutes the fountain pours out \(\boxed{72}\) liters.

blocks.x: So here is what we found. The fountain kept the same ratio the whole time, 9 liters for every 1 minute, whether we waited 2 minutes or 5 or 8. When a ratio stays steady like that no matter how big or small the amounts get, we say the two quantities are proportional. And the moment we set one of those equal ratios next to another, like \(\frac{9}{1} = \frac{x}{8}\), we have written a proportion, an equation that says two ratios are equal. That steady ratio is the whole heart of it, because once we know it holds, we can build a proportion to reach any new value we want.

At a summer camp, the kitchen has one rule it never breaks. Every \(2\) cups of dry rice feed exactly \(5\) hungry campers, since the rice is always cooked the same trusted way. That means the relationship between cups and campers is proportional, so the ratio of cups to campers stays the same no matter how big the group gets. Tonight the count comes in and \(15\) campers are walking up the hill for dinner. The cook needs to scale the recipe up without breaking that ratio. How many cups of dry rice are needed for \(15\) campers?
hints: Set two equal ratios side by side, keeping cups on top and campers on the bottom in both. That gives \(\frac{2}{5} = \frac{x}{15}\), where \(x\) is the cups you want. Now look at the camper side. What did \(5\) get multiplied by to become \(15\)?

Notice that \(5 \times 3 = 15\), so the campers grew by a factor of \(3\). To keep the ratio equal, the cups have to grow by that very same \(3\). If scaling feels shaky, cross-multiply instead, which turns \(\frac{2}{5} = \frac{x}{15}\) into \(5x = 2 \times 15\).
blocks.sol: Write the two ratios as a proportion, with cups on top and campers on the bottom in both, $$\frac{2}{5} = \frac{x}{15}.$$ The fastest path here is scaling, the same move from equivalent ratios. The campers went from \(5\) to \(15\), which is \(\times 3\), so the cups go up by the same \(\times 3\), giving \(2 \times 3 = 6\). To prove it with the new tool, cross-multiply. Setting the cross-products equal gives \(5x = 2 \times 15\), so \(5x = 30\). Divide both sides by \(5\) to get \(x = 6\). Check that the cross-products match, \(2 \times 15 = 30\) and \(5 \times 6 = 30\), so the ratios truly are equal. The cook needs \(\boxed{6}\) cups of dry rice.

blocks.x: Here is the move I keep reaching for. When one matching pair in a proportion is easy to compare, I find the number I multiply the known side by to land on the new side, then I do the very same multiplication to its partner, and the unknown just falls out. It is the equivalent-ratio idea from 7.1, only now I have written the two ratios as an equal-sign equation, so finding the multiplier and scaling the partner is the whole job. The catch is that this only feels quick when the multiplier jumps out at me, like a clean doubling or tripling. So I am already a little nervous about the day one part grows from \(14\) to \(20\), because there is no tidy whole-number jump to spot, and I will need a way that does not lean on seeing it.

A workshop mixes a custom paint by always pouring \(8\) small jars of pigment into \(14\) liters of base. The ratio never changes, so the mix is the same shade every time. A big order comes in that needs \(20\) small jars of pigment, and the painter wants to keep the same shade. Putting jars over liters on both sides gives the proportion \(\frac{8}{14} = \frac{20}{x}\), where \(x\) is the unknown amount of base. The jump from \(14\) to \(x\) is not a clean whole-number scale, so the usual eyeballing does not work here. How many liters of base are needed for \(20\) small jars?
hints: Write a proportion with the same units in the same spots on both sides. Keep jars on top and liters on the bottom, so \(\frac{8 \text{ jars}}{14 \text{ liters}} = \frac{20 \text{ jars}}{x \text{ liters}}\).
You can clear both denominators at once. Multiply each numerator by the other side's denominator, which gives \(8 \cdot x = 14 \cdot 20\), then solve the equation \(8x = 280\).
blocks.sol: Set up the proportion with jars on top and liters on the bottom in both ratios. $$\frac{8}{14} = \frac{20}{x}$$ Back in lesson 6.2 you cleared a fraction by multiplying the whole equation by its denominator. Do that for both denominators here. Multiply both sides by \(14\) and by \(x\). On the left the \(14\) cancels, and on the right the \(x\) cancels, leaving \(8 \cdot x\) on one side and \(14 \cdot 20\) on the other. $$8 \cdot x = 14 \cdot 20$$ That shortcut, where each numerator pairs with the other side's denominator, is called cross-multiplication. It works because it is just clearing the fractions like any ordinary equation. Now finish solving. $$8x = 280$$ $$x = \frac{280}{8} = 35$$ The big order needs \(\boxed{35}\) liters of base. Check the cross-products. \(8 \times 35 = 280\) and \(14 \times 20 = 280\), so they match.

The other arrow sweeps from the b in the bottom left up to the c in the top right and is labeled b times c. The two arrows land on the result a d equals b c written off to the right.
