blocks.cap: Cross-multiplying is not a new rule, it is the clearing step from Chapter 6 done in one go. Multiply both sides of \(\frac{a}{b} = \frac{c}{d}\) by \(b\) and by \(d\), the \(b\) cancels on the left and the \(d\) cancels on the right, and you are left with \(ad = bc\). The crossing X is just a memory aid for that move. You can always check it with numbers, since \(\frac{8}{14} = \frac{20}{35}\) is true, the cross products must match, and indeed \(8 \times 35 = 280\) and \(14 \times 20 = 280\).

blocks.x: A 3D printer squeezes out melted plastic at a steady, proportional rate, so the grams of filament and the layers built always keep the same ratio. During a calibration test, \(5\) grams of filament built \(8\) layers of a small model. Now you want to print a shorter version of the same model that is only \(6\) layers tall. Here is the snag. Going from \(8\) layers down to \(6\) layers, there is no clean whole-number you can multiply or divide by, so the scaling trick from earlier in the chapter stalls out. This is exactly where setting up a proportion and cross-multiplying earns its keep. How many grams of filament does the \(6\)-layer piece need?
hints: Both ratios compare the same two things, grams and layers, so keep grams on top and layers on the bottom in both fractions. The test gives \(\frac{5}{8}\), and the unknown grams over the \(6\) layers gives \(\frac{x}{6}\). Set them equal as \(\frac{5}{8} = \frac{x}{6}\).

You cannot scale \(8\) to \(6\) cleanly, so cross-multiply instead. Multiply each numerator by the other denominator to clear the fractions, which turns \(\frac{5}{8} = \frac{x}{6}\) into \(8x = 5 \times 6\). Then divide both sides by \(8\).
blocks.sol: Because the rate is proportional, grams and layers stay in the same ratio. Put grams on top and layers on the bottom in both fractions, with the known test on one side and the unknown grams \(x\) on the other. $$\frac{5}{8} = \frac{x}{6}$$ There is no whole-number multiplier from \(8\) to \(6\), so scaling stalls. Cross-multiply instead, which multiplies each numerator by the opposite denominator and clears both fractions. $$8 \times x = 5 \times 6$$ $$8x = 30$$ Now divide both sides by \(8\). $$x = \frac{30}{8} = \frac{15}{4}$$ Check the cross-products to be sure, \(5 \times 6 = 30\) and \(8 \times \frac{15}{4} = 30\), so the ratios truly match. As an exact decimal, \(\frac{15}{4} = 3.75\), so the \(6\)-layer piece needs \(\boxed{15/4}\) grams of filament.

blocks.x: Here is the move that cracks any proportion. When two ratios are set equal, \(\frac{a}{b} = \frac{c}{d}\), I can cross-multiply, which just means \(a\,d = b\,c\), and suddenly the fractions are gone and I am left with a plain equation like the ones from 6.2. It works because cross-multiplying is really multiplying both sides by both bottoms at once, so the denominators cancel and nothing about the balance changes. Scaling is faster when I can spot an easy whole-number jump, but cross-multiplication never needs a tidy jump, so it solves every proportion, even the ones whose answer turns out to be a fraction or a decimal.

A trail map promises that distances stay proportional everywhere on it, so the real-to-map ratio is the same no matter which two spots you pick. Two known overlooks sit \(6\) centimeters apart on the map and \(10\) kilometers apart on the actual trail. A different pair of overlooks is \(9\) kilometers apart on the actual trail, and you want to know how far apart they fall on the map. Set up real over map equals real over map so the unknown sits in a denominator, and notice that landing the letter on the bottom changes nothing about how you solve. How many centimeters apart are the two overlooks on the map?
hints: Write two ratios that must be equal, with real kilometers on top and map centimeters on the bottom in both. The first pair gives \(\frac{10}{6}\), and the second pair gives \(\frac{9}{x}\), so your proportion is \(\frac{10}{6} = \frac{9}{x}\) with the unknown sitting in a denominator.

Cross-multiply just like always, top times opposite bottom equals top times opposite bottom, which gives \(10x = 6 \times 9\). A denominator unknown is no harder, since cross-multiplication treats both slots the same way. Work out the right side, then divide both sides by \(10\) to free \(x\).
blocks.sol: Keep real kilometers on top and map centimeters on the bottom in both ratios, which puts the unknown map distance in a denominator. $$\frac{10}{6} = \frac{9}{x}$$ Cross-multiply, top times opposite bottom on each side. $$10x = 6 \times 9 = 54$$ The unknown was on the bottom, but cross-multiplication treated it exactly like a numerator, and now one division finishes the job. Divide both sides by \(10\). $$x = \frac{54}{10} = \frac{27}{5}$$ As a decimal that is \(5.4\) centimeters, which is sensible since \(9\) kilometers is a bit less than the \(10\) kilometers that spanned \(6\) centimeters. The two overlooks are \(\boxed{27/5}\) centimeters apart on the map.

blocks.x: A scale model of a sailboat keeps every length proportional to the real boat. On the model, \(3\) inches of mast stand in for \(90\) inches of real mast, so the model shrinks the real boat by a constant ratio. The boom appears as \(5\) inches on the model, and you want its true length. A student in a hurry scribbles \(\frac{3}{90} = \frac{x}{5}\), letting \(x\) be the real boom. Notice the trouble. On the left the top is model inches and the bottom is real inches, but on the right \(x\) is the real length sitting on top. The two sides do not read the same kind of rate, and that scramble leads to a nonsense answer. Set the proportion up so matching units sit in matching positions, then solve. What is the real length of the boom, in inches?
hints: Keep matching units in matching positions so each side reads the same rate. Put real inches on top and model inches on the bottom for both ratios. The mast gives \(\frac{90}{3}\), and the boom gives \(\frac{x}{5}\), so write \(\frac{90}{3} = \frac{x}{5}\).
Clear the fractions by cross-multiplying. From \(\frac{90}{3} = \frac{x}{5}\) you get \(3x = 90 \times 5\). Work out the right side, then divide both sides by \(3\) to find \(x\).
blocks.sol: Match the units before you write anything. Put real inches on top and model inches on the bottom for both ratios, so each side is the same kind of rate. The mast tells you the rate, \(\frac{90 \text{ real}}{3 \text{ model}}\), and the boom follows the same rate, \(\frac{x \text{ real}}{5 \text{ model}}\). Setting them equal gives the proportion $$\frac{90}{3} = \frac{x}{5}.$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators, which gives \(3x = 90 \times 5\), so \(3x = 450\). Divide both sides by \(3\) to get \(x = 150\). Check the matching positions, real over model equals real over model, and the cross-products agree since \(90 \times 5 = 450\) and \(3 \times 150 = 450\). The real boom is \(\boxed{150}\) inches long.

blocks.x: After the sailboat I finally see what to check before I cross-multiply. The proportion only works if the matching units sit in matching spots, so the top of one side and the top of the other measure the same kind of thing, and the bottoms do too. Then both sides really are the same rate, real over model equals real over model, or miles over inches equals miles over inches. If I flip one side by accident, cross-multiplication still does its job, it just faithfully multiplies the wrong quantities and hands me an answer that can't be right. So I read both sides out loud first and make sure they describe the same rate.

A little exchange kiosk on a harbor swaps dollars for the island's painted shells, and it posts one fixed rate that stays the same all day. Because the rate never changes, dollars and shells stay proportional. In the morning a traveler hands over \(7\) dollars and gets back \(91\) shells. In the afternoon she comes back and wants to change \(11\) dollars at that same posted rate. How many shells does she get for \(11\) dollars?
hints: The rate is the same both times, so the ratio of dollars to shells is constant. Write one proportion with dollars on top and shells on the bottom in both fractions, \(\frac{7}{91} = \frac{11}{x}\), so the matching units line up.
Now cross-multiply. Multiply \(7\) by \(x\) and set it equal to \(91 \times 11\), then divide to get \(x\) by itself.
blocks.sol: Since the kiosk uses one fixed rate, dollars and shells are proportional, so the morning trade and the afternoon trade form a single proportion. Keep dollars on top and shells on the bottom in both fractions so the units match. $$\frac{7 \text{ dollars}}{91 \text{ shells}} = \frac{11 \text{ dollars}}{x \text{ shells}}$$ Cross-multiply, which clears both denominators and leaves a plain equation. $$7 \cdot x = 91 \cdot 11$$ $$7x = 1001$$ Divide both sides by \(7\). $$x = \frac{1001}{7} = 143$$ You can check it. The first trade gives \(\frac{7}{91} = \frac{1}{13}\), one dollar for thirteen shells, and \(\frac{11}{143} = \frac{1}{13}\) is the same rate. She gets \(\boxed{143}\) shells.

blocks.x: A trail-snack recipe keeps the same taste by fixing the ratio of oats to crushed cashews at \(5\) scoops of oats for every \(2\) scoops of cashews. A scout troop wants a bigger batch built on \(12\) scoops of oats, and they need to know how many scoops of cashews to add so the mix tastes exactly the same. Because oats and cashews stay in a constant ratio, you can set oats over cashews equal to oats over cashews, \(\frac{5}{2} = \frac{12}{x}\), where \(x\) is the unknown cashew amount. How many scoops of cashews keep the ratio right?
hints: Write the proportion with matching units in matching spots. Put oats on top and cashews on the bottom on both sides, \(\frac{5}{2} = \frac{12}{x}\). The recipe ratio sits on the left, and the big batch sits on the right with the unknown cashews as \(x\).

Jumping from \(5\) oats to \(12\) oats is not a clean whole-number scale, so cross-multiply instead of guessing. Multiply each top by the opposite bottom to get \(5 \cdot x = 2 \cdot 12\), then solve for \(x\).
blocks.sol: Set up the proportion with oats on top and cashews on the bottom on both sides, $$\frac{5}{2} = \frac{12}{x}.$$ The scale from \(5\) to \(12\) is not a whole number, so cross-multiply to clear the denominators. Multiply each numerator by the opposite denominator, $$5 \cdot x = 2 \cdot 12.$$ That gives \(5x = 24\). Divide both sides by \(5\) to get \(x = \frac{24}{5}\), which is the exact decimal \(4.8\). Check the ratio, \(\frac{12}{4.8} = \frac{5}{2}\), so the taste stays the same. The troop needs \(\boxed{24/5}\) scoops of cashews.

<svg viewBox="0 0 440 332" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A shadow figure showing how a known pole measures an unknown tree. A flat ground line runs across the bottom. On the left stands a short vertical pole 3 feet tall casting a 4 foot shadow along the ground. On the right stands a tall tree of unknown height h casting a 12 foot shadow. Two parallel slanted rays of sunlight, coming from the upper right, strike the top of the pole and the top of the tree and continue down to the far end of each shadow. Each object together with its shadow and its ray forms a right triangle. The small pole triangle and the large tree triangle have the same shape, they are similar, because the sun's rays are parallel. Below the picture the proportion height over shadow equals height over shadow is written as three over four equals h over twelve, with a note that both triangles share the one constant ratio three fourths, which is why a known pole can measure an unknown tree from shadows alone.">

  <text x="86" y="291" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="10.5" fill="var(--slate)">height</text>
  <line x1="58" y1="298" x2="114" y2="298" stroke="var(--ink)" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="86" y="311" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="10.5" fill="var(--slate)">shadow</text>
  <text x="86" y="327" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="9.5" fill="var(--mid)">= 3/4</text>
  <text x="140" y="306" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="15" fill="var(--ink)">=</text>
  <text x="200" y="291" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="10.5" fill="var(--slate)">height</text>
  <line x1="170" y1="298" x2="230" y2="298" stroke="var(--ink)" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="200" y="311" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="10.5" fill="var(--slate)">shadow</text>
  <text x="200" y="327" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="9.5" fill="var(--mid)">= h/12</text>
  <rect x="262" y="282" width="156" height="40" rx="6" fill="var(--softer)" stroke="var(--gold2)" stroke-width="1.4"/>
  <text x="340" y="298" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="var(--mid)">one ratio for both</text>
  <text x="340" y="314" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="700" fill="var(--gold-deep)">3/4</text>
</svg>
blocks.cap: At the same moment the short pole and the tall tree make right triangles with their shadows, and because the sun's rays come in parallel, the two triangles are the same shape, just sized differently. Same shape means one shared ratio of height to shadow, here \(\frac{3}{4}\), so the unknown tree must obey it too, giving \(\frac{3}{4} = \frac{h}{12}\). That single constant ratio is exactly why a pole you can measure lets you find a tree you cannot reach, using only the shadows on the ground.

blocks.x: Late one sunny afternoon, every upright object on a level field throws a shadow at the same slant, because the sun's rays strike everything from the same angle. A park ranger holds a measuring rod straight up and finds that the \(6\)-foot rod casts a shadow \(8\) feet long. Across the field, a fire-watch antenna casts a shadow \(28\) feet long at that same instant. Since the rays hit both at one angle, the two right triangles formed by object, shadow, and ray have the same shape, so their height-to-shadow ratios are equal and the antenna's height is proportional to its shadow. How tall is the fire-watch antenna, in feet?
hints: The rod and the antenna make the same shape, so height over shadow is the same for both. Write one ratio for the rod and set it equal to the same kind of ratio for the antenna, keeping height on top and shadow on the bottom in both. That gives \(\frac{6}{8} = \frac{h}{28}\).
Now clear the fractions by cross-multiplying. Multiply the \(6\) by the \(28\) and set it equal to \(8\) times \(h\), then divide both sides to get \(h\) by itself.
blocks.sol: Both objects stand straight up and their rays slant the same way, so the height-to-shadow ratio is the same for each. Put the rod's ratio equal to the antenna's ratio, with height on top and shadow on the bottom in both spots. $$\frac{6}{8} = \frac{h}{28}$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators, multiplying each top by the other bottom. $$8 \cdot h = 6 \cdot 28$$ That gives \(8h = 168\). Divide both sides by \(8\). $$h = \frac{168}{8} = 21$$ The fire-watch antenna stands \(\boxed{21}\) feet tall.

blocks.x: Around 2,600 years ago the Greek thinker Thales of Miletus is said to have found the height of a great Egyptian pyramid without ever climbing it. As the tradition goes, he watched an upright stick until the moment its shadow stretched exactly as long as the stick was tall, then he measured the pyramid's shadow at that same instant, reasoning that the pyramid's height had to match its shadow's length too. The deeper idea is the very one in this lesson, since the sun strikes the stick and the pyramid at the same angle, it makes similar triangles, so height over shadow stays a constant ratio. That constant ratio is a proportion you can write down and solve, exactly like the shadow problem you just worked. Treat the colorful details as legend, because the story reaches us only through writers who lived long after Thales, but the proportional-triangles reasoning behind it is completely sound. People have been turning shadows into measuring tapes for a very long time.

At a weekend craft fair, one stall sells handmade glass beads in small bundles, all at the same fixed price. A sign says 6 bundles cost 15 dollars, and since the price is steady, the cost stays proportional to the number of bundles. You decide to buy 10 bundles for a friend. How many dollars do 10 bundles cost?
hints: Two ratios that match describe the same fixed price, so set them equal. Keep the units lined up, bundles over dollars on each side, with the unknown cost as \(x\), which gives \(\frac{6}{15} = \frac{10}{x}\).
Clear the fractions by cross-multiplying. Multiply each numerator by the other side's denominator, so \(6 \cdot x = 15 \cdot 10\), then divide to get \(x\) by itself.
blocks.sol: Write the known price and the wanted price as two equal ratios, keeping bundles over dollars in both positions so the units match. Let \(x\) be the cost of 10 bundles. $$\frac{6}{15} = \frac{10}{x}$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators, which gives \(6 \cdot x = 15 \cdot 10\), so \(6x = 150\). Divide both sides by 6 to find \(x = \frac{150}{6} = 25\). You can check this the unit-rate way from 7.3. One bundle costs \(15 \div 6 = 2.5\) dollars, and 10 of them cost \(2.5 \times 10 = 25\) dollars. Both routes land on the same number, so 10 bundles cost \(\boxed{25}\) dollars.

blocks.x: A proportion is just two equal rates set side by side, so cross-multiplying is not the only honest way through. I can find the per-one value first, the unit rate from before, and then scale it up to however much I actually want. Both routes land on the same answer every time, so I get to pick whichever one fits the numbers in front of me. Cross-multiplication is my dependable workhorse, and the unit-rate route is the easy one when one side divides cleanly into a nice per-one amount.

A dashed connector runs from the map to the right side, where the same two places are drawn far apart along a real coastline with a long arrow marked x kilometers for the true distance. Below everything the scale is written as one proportion, 2 over 35 equals 6 over x, with the map measurement of 6 centimeters plugged into the top of the right fraction, showing that one fixed scale turns any map distance into a real distance through a single proportion.">

blocks.cap: A scale is a single fixed ratio, so we never need a chain of unit conversions here, just one proportion. Writing map centimeters over real kilometers, the scale \(2 \text{ cm} = 35 \text{ km}\) becomes \(\frac{2}{35}\), and the measured gap of \(6\) cm becomes \(\frac{6}{x}\) with the same units stacked the same way. Setting them equal gives the proportion \(\frac{2}{35} = \frac{6}{x}\), and once the \(6\) cm we measured is in place, solving for \(x\) turns that map length straight into the real distance.

blocks.x: A hiking map is drawn at a fixed scale where every \(2\) centimeters on the paper stands for \(35\) real kilometers of trail. On the map, two campsites sit \(6\) centimeters apart. Because the scale never changes, the ratio of map distance to real distance stays the same everywhere on the map, so you can match it against the campsite distance with a single proportion. How many kilometers apart are the two campsites in real life?
hints: The scale gives you one ratio of map centimeters to real kilometers, and the campsites give you another. Set them equal with the same units in the same spots, map-cm on top and real-km on the bottom, so \(\frac{2}{35} = \frac{6}{x}\).
Now cross-multiply to clear the fractions. The two cross-products must be equal, so \(2x = 35 \times 6\). Work out the right side, then divide both sides by \(2\) to find \(x\).
blocks.sol: Keep map centimeters on top and real kilometers on the bottom in both ratios so the units line up. The scale ratio is \(\frac{2}{35}\), and the campsite ratio is \(\frac{6}{x}\), where \(x\) is the real distance in kilometers. Setting them equal gives the proportion $$\frac{2}{35} = \frac{6}{x}.$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators, which makes the two cross-products equal. $$2 \cdot x = 35 \cdot 6$$ $$2x = 210$$ Divide both sides by \(2\). $$x = \frac{210}{2} = 105$$ The campsites are \(\boxed{105}\) kilometers apart in real life.

blocks.x: The art club designed a small banner on the computer, and the printer can blow it up to any size as long as it keeps the picture the same shape. To keep the shape, the width and the height have to scale together, so the ratio of width to height stays the same. The small design is \(8\) inches wide and \(5\) inches tall. The printed banner will be \(20\) inches wide. The club wants the height that keeps the picture from looking stretched, so the width-to-height ratio of the banner matches the width-to-height ratio of the design. What is the height of the printed banner in inches?
hints: Keep the same kind of measurement in the same spot. Put width over height for both pictures and set them equal, so \(\frac{8}{5} = \frac{20}{x}\), where \(x\) is the height you want.
Cross-multiply to clear the fractions. From \(\frac{8}{5} = \frac{20}{x}\) you get \(8x = 5 \cdot 20\), then divide both sides by \(8\) to find \(x\).
blocks.sol: Match the units in matching positions. Both ratios are width over height, so the design gives \(\frac{8}{5}\) and the banner gives \(\frac{20}{x}\), and setting them equal gives the proportion $$\frac{8}{5} = \frac{20}{x}.$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators, which gives \(8x = 5 \cdot 20\), so \(8x = 100\). Divide both sides by \(8\) to get \(x = \frac{100}{8} = \frac{25}{2}\). As a quick check, that ratio equals the exact decimal \(12.5\) inches, and the cross-products match since \(8 \cdot \frac{25}{2} = 100\) and \(5 \cdot 20 = 100\). The height of the printed banner is \(\boxed{25/2}\).

blocks.x: An artist paints a small rectangular mural on tile, then makes a scaled-up copy for the wall of a community center. To keep the picture looking right, the big copy has to be the same shape as the small one, just larger, so the two rectangles are similar. The small mural is \(5\) feet tall and \(12\) feet wide. The big copy is built \(20\) feet tall. The artist needs to know how wide to make it so the shape stays true and nothing looks stretched. Here is the trap. It is tempting to compare height to width, but matching positions go together, so small height pairs with big height and small width pairs with big width. How many feet wide is the big copy of the mural?
hints: Keep matching positions in matching positions. Put the two heights together and the two widths together, so the small mural's height over its width equals the big copy's height over its width. With \(x\) as the unknown width, that is \(\frac{5}{12} = \frac{20}{x}\).

Now clear the fractions by cross-multiplying. The product across one diagonal equals the product across the other, so \(5 \cdot x = 12 \cdot 20\). Work out the right side, then divide both sides by \(5\) to get \(x\).
blocks.sol: Because the two rectangles are similar, their height-to-width ratios are equal. Keep matching positions together, small height over small width equals big height over big width, and let \(x\) be the unknown width. $$\frac{5}{12} = \frac{20}{x}$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators. $$5 \cdot x = 12 \cdot 20$$ The right side is \(240\), so \(5x = 240\). Divide both sides by \(5\). $$x = \frac{240}{5} = 48$$ Check the pairing, \(\frac{5}{12}\) and \(\frac{20}{48}\) both reduce to \(\frac{5}{12}\), so the shape stays true. The big copy is \(\boxed{48}\) feet wide.

review.x: A community center mixes a fruit punch that keeps juice and water proportional, using 4 cups of juice for every 10 cups of water. The volunteers want a bigger batch with the same taste, so they pour in 6 cups of juice. Setting up juice over water equal to juice over water gives \(\frac{4}{10} = \frac{6}{x}\). How many cups of water keep the same ratio?
hints: The two ratios are equal, so cross-multiply the proportion \(\frac{4}{10} = \frac{6}{x}\). Multiply the top of each fraction by the bottom of the other.
Cross-multiplying gives \(4 \cdot x = 10 \cdot 6\), so \(4x = 60\). Divide both sides by 4 to find x.
review.sol: Both batches must hold the same juice to water ratio, so set the two ratios equal with the unknown water as x. Keep juice on top and water on the bottom in both fractions. $$\frac{4}{10} = \frac{6}{x}$$ Cross-multiply, which means multiply each numerator by the other denominator. $$4 \cdot x = 10 \cdot 6$$ $$4x = 60$$ Divide both sides by 4. $$x = \frac{60}{4} = 15$$ Check the cross-products to be sure. \(4 \cdot 15 = 60\) and \(10 \cdot 6 = 60\), which match, so the ratio holds. The bigger batch needs \(\boxed{15}\) cups of water.

review.x: A small theater company mixes a custom stage paint that always keeps the same blend, 3 ounces of deep teal pigment for every 7 ounces of base. They want to mix a bigger batch that uses 21 ounces of base. To keep the color exact, they set up \(\frac{3}{7} = \frac{x}{21}\), where \(x\) is the pigment that goes with 21 ounces of base. How many ounces of teal pigment do they need?
hints: The base went from 7 ounces to 21 ounces. Figure out what number you multiply 7 by to get 21, since the same color means both sides scale by that same factor.
You multiplied the base by 3 to go from 7 to 21. Do the exact same thing to the pigment, so multiply 3 by 3.
review.sol: Start from the proportion that keeps the color constant, \(\frac{3}{7} = \frac{x}{21}\). Cross-multiply to clear the denominators, which gives \(7 \cdot x = 3 \cdot 21\), so \(7x = 63\). Divide both sides by 7 to get \(x = \frac{63}{7} = 9\). You can sanity check by scaling. The base grew from 7 to 21, a clean \(\times 3\), and the pigment grows the same way, \(3 \times 3 = 9\). They need \(\boxed{9}\) ounces of teal pigment.

review.x: A pottery studio mixes glaze using a fixed ratio of 6 scoops of clay powder for every 5 cups of water. The studio has a big batch of 40 cups of water ready to use. How many scoops of clay powder are needed to keep the same ratio?
hints: The ratio of clay powder to water stays constant. Write \(\frac{6}{5} = \frac{x}{40}\) with scoops on top and cups on the bottom in both fractions.
Cross-multiply to clear the denominators. That gives \(5x = 6 \times 40\), so divide both sides by 5.
review.sol: Keep the ratio of scoops of clay powder to cups of water the same. Let \(x\) be the scoops needed for 40 cups, so $$\frac{6}{5} = \frac{x}{40}.$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators, giving \(5x = 6 \times 40\), so \(5x = 240\). Divide both sides by 5 to get \(x = 48\). The batch needs \(\boxed{48}\) scoops of clay powder.

review.x: A small bakery follows a proportional yeast recipe, using \(7\) grams of yeast for every \(10\) dinner rolls. The head baker wants to make just \(4\) rolls for a tasting and needs to scale the same recipe down. How many grams of yeast are needed for \(4\) rolls?
hints: The ratio of grams to rolls stays constant. Set up grams over rolls equal to grams over rolls, so \(\frac{7}{10} = \frac{x}{4}\), keeping grams on top in both fractions.
Cross-multiply to clear the denominators. That gives \(10x = 7 \times 4\), then divide both sides by \(10\).
review.sol: The grams of yeast per roll never changes, so the two ratios are equal. Put grams on top and rolls on the bottom in both fractions, which gives \(\frac{7}{10} = \frac{x}{4}\). Cross-multiply to clear the denominators, so \(10x = 7 \times 4\), which is \(10x = 28\). Divide both sides by \(10\) to get \(x = \frac{28}{10}\). Reduce that fraction by dividing top and bottom by \(2\), leaving \(x = \frac{14}{5}\), the same as \(2.8\) grams. So the bakery needs \(\boxed{14/5}\) grams of yeast for \(4\) rolls.

review.x: A perfume lab mixes oils so the ratio of rose oil to sandalwood oil is always the same. One sample they trust holds 12 drops of rose oil for every 21 drops of sandalwood oil. A small tester vial keeps that same ratio but uses only 8 drops of rose oil. How many drops of sandalwood oil are in the tester vial?
hints: The ratio of rose oil to sandalwood oil stays constant, so set the two ratios equal with rose on top in both, giving \(\frac{12}{21} = \frac{8}{x}\).
Cross-multiply to clear the fractions. That turns \(\frac{12}{21} = \frac{8}{x}\) into \(12x = 21 \times 8\), then divide to find \(x\).
review.sol: Both mixes keep the same ratio of rose oil to sandalwood oil, so write a proportion with rose on top and sandalwood on the bottom in each fraction. $$\frac{12}{21} = \frac{8}{x}$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators. $$12 \cdot x = 21 \cdot 8$$ $$12x = 168$$ Divide both sides by \(12\). $$x = \frac{168}{12} = 14$$ The tester vial holds \(\boxed{14}\) drops of sandalwood oil.

review.x: A botanical garden grows two flower beds with the same mix of tulips to daffodils. The first bed holds 9 tulips for every 12 daffodils. The second bed keeps that same ratio and has 15 tulips. How many daffodils are in the second bed?
hints: Both beds share the same tulip to daffodil ratio, so set the two ratios equal as a proportion with tulips on top and daffodils on the bottom in both fractions. Let \(x\) be the daffodils in the second bed.
Cross-multiply to clear the fractions. From \(\frac{9}{12} = \frac{15}{x}\) you get \(9x = 12 \times 15\), then divide both sides by \(9\).
review.sol: Put tulips on top and daffodils on the bottom in both fractions so the units match in matching positions. Let \(x\) be the number of daffodils in the second bed. $$\frac{9}{12} = \frac{15}{x}$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators. $$9 \cdot x = 12 \cdot 15$$ $$9x = 180$$ Divide both sides by \(9\). $$x = \frac{180}{9} = 20$$ The second bed has \(\boxed{20}\) daffodils.

review.x: A tiny exchange window at a board game cafe trades real dollars for plastic arcade tokens at one fixed rate, and the ratio of dollars to tokens never changes no matter how much you swap. You watch the person ahead of you hand over \(5\) dollars and get back \(65\) tokens. You step up with \(8\) dollars. How many tokens do you get for \(8\) dollars?
hints: The rate stays constant, so dollars over tokens for the first swap equals dollars over tokens for your swap. Put the matching units in matching spots, \(\frac{5}{65} = \frac{8}{x}\).
Cross-multiply to clear the fractions. That turns \(\frac{5}{65} = \frac{8}{x}\) into \(5x = 65 \times 8\), then divide both sides by \(5\).
review.sol:
Because the rate is fixed, the ratio of dollars to tokens is the same for both swaps. Keep dollars on top and tokens on the bottom in both ratios.

$$\frac{5}{65} = \frac{8}{x}$$

Cross-multiply to clear the denominators.

$$5 \times x = 65 \times 8$$

$$5x = 520$$

Divide both sides by \(5\).

$$x = \frac{520}{5} = 104$$

So \(8\) dollars gives you \(\boxed{104}\) tokens.

review.x: A pancake stand keeps its batter consistent by mixing flour and sugar in the same proportion every time, 5 cups of flour for every 2 cups of sugar. The cook is making a giant weekend batch that calls for 12 cups of sugar, and she needs to scale the flour to match so the batter tastes right. How many cups of flour does the giant batch need?
hints: The ratio of flour to sugar stays constant. Write one ratio from the recipe and set it equal to a ratio for the big batch, keeping flour on top and sugar on the bottom in both, giving \(\frac{5}{2} = \frac{x}{12}\).
Cross-multiply to clear the fractions. That turns \(\frac{5}{2} = \frac{x}{12}\) into \(2x = 5 \times 12\), then divide to find \(x\).
review.sol: The flour to sugar ratio stays the same, so set the recipe ratio equal to the big-batch ratio with flour on top and sugar on the bottom in both places. $$\frac{5}{2} = \frac{x}{12}$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators. $$2x = 5 \times 12$$ $$2x = 60$$ Divide both sides by \(2\). $$x = \frac{60}{2} = 30$$ The giant batch needs \(\boxed{30}\) cups of flour.

review.x: On a hiking map, a scale tells you that \(2\) centimeters stands for \(45\) kilometers of real trail. Two ranger stations sit \(5\) centimeters apart on the map. How many kilometers apart are the two stations in real life?
hints: The map distance and the real distance keep a constant ratio, so write map-cm over real-km on both sides and let \(x\) be the real kilometers you want.
Set \(\frac{2}{45} = \frac{5}{x}\), then cross-multiply to turn it into \(2x = 45 \times 5\).
review.sol: Keep map centimeters on top and real kilometers on the bottom in both ratios, with \(x\) standing for the real distance you are after. $$\frac{2}{45} = \frac{5}{x}$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators. $$2 \cdot x = 45 \cdot 5$$ $$2x = 225$$ Divide both sides by \(2\). $$x = \frac{225}{2} = 112.5$$ So the two stations are \(\boxed{112.5}\) kilometers apart in real life.

review.x: A garden gnome standing \(4\) feet tall casts a shadow \(5\) feet long. At that same minute, a nearby water tower casts a shadow \(50\) feet long. The Sun is in the same spot for both, so the ratio of height to shadow length stays constant for every object on the lawn. How tall is the water tower in feet?
hints: The height-to-shadow ratio is the same for both objects, so put the gnome's height over its shadow on one side and the tower's height over its shadow on the other. Let \(h\) be the tower's height.
You should have \(\frac{4}{5} = \frac{h}{50}\). Cross-multiply to get \(5h = 4 \times 50\), then divide both sides by \(5\).
review.sol: Both objects share the same height-to-shadow ratio, so I set up a proportion with height on top and shadow length on the bottom in both spots. Letting \(h\) be the tower's height, $$\frac{4}{5} = \frac{h}{50}.$$ Cross-multiplying clears the denominators, \(5h = 4 \times 50\), so \(5h = 200\). Dividing both sides by \(5\) gives \(h = 40\). The water tower is \(\boxed{40}\) feet tall.

review.x: A delivery van keeps a steady speed on a long flat highway, so the distance it covers stays proportional to the time it spends driving. In its first stretch it covers \(60\) miles in \(4\) hours. If it holds that same speed, how many miles does the van cover in \(7\) hours?
hints: The speed is constant, so the ratio of miles to hours stays the same. Put miles on top and hours on the bottom in both fractions, with the unknown distance over \(7\) hours.
You should have \(\frac{60}{4} = \frac{x}{7}\). Cross-multiply to clear the denominators, then divide to get \(x\) by itself.
review.sol: Since the van holds a steady speed, miles and hours stay in a constant ratio, so I set up a proportion with miles over hours on both sides. $$\frac{60 \text{ miles}}{4 \text{ hours}} = \frac{x \text{ miles}}{7 \text{ hours}}$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators. $$4x = 60 \times 7$$ $$4x = 420$$ Now divide both sides by \(4\). $$x = \frac{420}{4} = 105$$ So in \(7\) hours the van covers \(\boxed{105}\) miles.

review.x: A glassworker is reproducing a small stained-glass panel at a larger size for a library window, and she keeps the new panel the exact same shape as the original so the picture is not stretched. Because the shape is preserved, the width and the height stay proportional, meaning width compared to height is the same comparison in both panels. The original panel is \(6\) inches wide and \(9\) inches tall. The enlarged panel is \(22\) inches wide. How tall is the enlarged panel, in inches?
hints: Keeping the shape means width over height is the same comparison in both panels. Put the original on one side and the enlargement on the other, with width on top and height on the bottom in both. That gives \(\frac{6}{9} = \frac{22}{x}\), where \(x\) is the height you want.
Cross-multiply the proportion \(\frac{6}{9} = \frac{22}{x}\) to clear the denominators. That turns it into \(6x = 9 \times 22\). Work out the right side, then divide both sides by \(6\).
review.sol: Keeping the panel the same shape means the width-to-height comparison cannot change, so the two panels form a proportion. Write width on top and height on the bottom for both, the original on the left and the enlargement on the right. $$\frac{6}{9} = \frac{22}{x}$$ Here \(x\) is the height of the enlarged panel in inches. To solve, cross-multiply, which clears both denominators just like in equation solving. Multiply the top of each side by the bottom of the other. $$6 \times x = 9 \times 22$$ The right side is \(9 \times 22 = 198\), so the equation becomes \(6x = 198\). Now divide both sides by \(6\) to get \(x\) by itself. $$x = \frac{198}{6} = 33$$ A quick check shows the comparison held. The enlargement is \(22:33\), and dividing both parts by \(11\) gives \(2:3\), the very same shape as \(6:9\). So the enlarged panel is \(\boxed{33}\) inches tall.

review.x: A sourdough starter bubbles best when the baker keeps a steady ratio of starter to fresh flour. Today's jar uses \(3\) grams of starter for every \(8\) grams of flour. Tomorrow the baker plans to mix in exactly \(5\) grams of fresh flour and wants to hold that same starter-to-flour ratio. How many grams of starter should go into the jar?
hints: The ratio of starter to flour stays constant, so set the two ratios equal with starter on top and flour on the bottom in both, giving \(\frac{3}{8} = \frac{x}{5}\).
Cross-multiply to clear the denominators. That turns \(\frac{3}{8} = \frac{x}{5}\) into \(8x = 3 \times 5\), then divide both sides by \(8\).
review.sol: Keep starter on top and flour on the bottom in both ratios, so the proportion is $$\frac{3}{8} = \frac{x}{5}.$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators, which gives \(8x = 3 \times 5\), so \(8x = 15\). Divide both sides by \(8\) to get \(x = \frac{15}{8}\). As a check, \(\frac{15}{8} = 1.875\) grams of starter, and the cross-products match since \(3 \times 5 = 15\) and \(8 \times \frac{15}{8} = 15\). So the baker needs \(\boxed{15/8}\) grams of starter.

review.x: A glassblower sketches two similar triangular sails for a model boat, a small one and an enlarged copy. On the small sail, the bottom edge is \(12\) centimeters and the slanted edge is \(8\) centimeters. The enlarged sail keeps the same shape, so the ratio of bottom edge to slanted edge stays constant. Its bottom edge measures \(27\) centimeters. How long is the slanted edge of the enlarged sail, in centimeters?
hints: The two sails are similar, so bottom edge over slanted edge is the same for both. Set up \(\frac{12}{8} = \frac{27}{x}\), keeping bottom edges on top and slanted edges on the bottom.
Cross-multiply to clear the fractions. That turns \(\frac{12}{8} = \frac{27}{x}\) into \(12x = 8 \times 27\), then divide to find \(x\).
review.sol: Because the sails are similar, the bottom edge and the slanted edge keep a constant ratio. Put bottom edges on top and slanted edges on the bottom in both ratios. $$\frac{12}{8} = \frac{27}{x}$$ Cross-multiply to clear the denominators. $$12x = 8 \times 27$$ $$12x = 216$$ Divide both sides by \(12\). $$x = \frac{216}{12} = 18$$ The slanted edge of the enlarged sail is \(\boxed{18}\) centimeters.

blocks.x: This is the last lesson of the chapter, so let me close it out by handing you the very thing every lesson has been building toward. All chapter long you compared two amounts and rescaled them, you boiled rates down to a single unit, and in 7.4 you balanced two ratios across an equals sign and cross-multiplied to crack them open. Now I want to point at one special ratio that has been hiding in plain sight the whole time. When the top and the bottom of a fraction are the same real amount written in two different units, that fraction equals \(1\). Look at \(\frac{12 \text{ in}}{1 \text{ ft}}\). Twelve inches is not close to one foot, it is one foot, the exact same length spelled two ways, so that fraction is just one amount divided by itself, which is \(1\). A ratio like that, equal to \(1\) because its top and bottom are the same amount in two units, is called a conversion factor. Here is why it is so quietly powerful. Multiplying any quantity by something equal to \(1\) cannot change the real amount, the way multiplying by \(1\) never changes a number. So a conversion factor leaves the amount untouched and only changes the units it is dressed in. That is the one idea to carry through this whole lesson, multiplying by \(1\) changes the costume, not the amount. The length, the weight, the speed stays exactly what it was, it just answers to a different unit name afterward. And notice this is not a brand new tool. A conversion factor is simply one of the equivalent ratios you already trust, the kind you could drop into a proportion from 7.4, except now it has a steady job, turning one unit into another. Here is the path ahead. First you will build these conversion factors from any relationship you are given, then flip each one to the orientation that cancels the unit you do not want and leaves the unit you do. You will do single conversions, chain several together through in-between units, and stretch the method to rates and to square units. To finish, you will convert quantities using units we invent ourselves, just to prove the method does not care what the units mean, it only cares that the costume change is honest.

Horse and pony people measure height in a unit called hands, and the rule never changes, 1 hand is exactly 4 inches. A young pony stands 36 inches tall at the shoulder. Here is the quiet trick that makes every conversion work. The fraction \(\frac{4 \text{ in}}{1 \text{ hand}}\) is really just \(1\), because 4 inches and 1 hand are the very same height written two ways, and any amount over itself equals \(1\). Multiplying by \(1\) never changes a value, so you can multiply the pony's height by that fraction without changing how tall the pony is. Flip it so inches sits on the bottom where it will cancel, which gives \(36 \text{ in} \times \frac{1 \text{ hand}}{4 \text{ in}}\). The inches cancel and hands are left behind. How many hands tall is the pony?
hints: A conversion factor is a fraction equal to \(1\) whose top and bottom are the same height in different units, so both \(\frac{4 \text{ in}}{1 \text{ hand}}\) and \(\frac{1 \text{ hand}}{4 \text{ in}}\) equal \(1\). You start with inches and want hands, so choose the one with inches on the bottom, \(\frac{1 \text{ hand}}{4 \text{ in}}\). That way inches will cancel and hands will be the unit that survives.

Write the chain as \(36 \text{ in} \times \frac{1 \text{ hand}}{4 \text{ in}}\). Cross out the \(\text{in}\) on top against the \(\text{in}\) on the bottom, then the unit left is hands. Now just do the number part, \(36 \div 4\).
blocks.sol: Start with the height you know and multiply by the conversion factor that puts inches on the bottom, so inches can cancel and hands remain. $$36 \text{ in} \times \frac{1 \text{ hand}}{4 \text{ in}} = \frac{36 \cancel{\text{ in}}}{1} \times \frac{1 \text{ hand}}{4 \cancel{\text{ in}}}$$ The inches cancel, leaving only hands as the unit. Now handle the numbers, \(\frac{36}{4} = 9\), so the pony is \(9\) hands tall. $$36 \div 4 = \boxed{9}$$

blocks.x: So here is what we keep doing. A conversion factor is a fraction whose top and bottom are the exact same real amount, just written in two different units, which means the fraction is secretly equal to \(1\). And multiplying any quantity by \(1\) cannot change how much you have, so the real amount sits there untouched while the only thing that swaps is the unit it is wearing. The pony never shrank and never grew an inch. We just took its height out of the costume marked "inches" and slipped it into the one marked "hands", and the pony underneath stayed exactly the same size.

Out on the open water, sailors measure depth in fathoms, and one fathom is exactly 6 feet. A diver lowers an anchor rope over the side and lets out the whole thing, which is 23 fathoms long. To compare it against markings on the deck, the diver needs that length in feet. Build a conversion factor by writing the relationship as the fraction \(\frac{6 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ fathom}}\). Since the top and bottom are the same amount, that fraction equals \(1\), so multiplying by it never changes the true length, it only changes the unit. Set up \(23 \text{ fathom} \times \frac{6 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ fathom}}\) and notice that "fathom" appears on top in the first factor and on the bottom in the second, so it cancels and "ft" is what survives. How many feet long is the rope?
hints: Write the relationship as a fraction equal to \(1\). You want "fathom" to cancel, so the fathom unit needs to sit on the bottom of your conversion factor while feet sits on top, giving \(\frac{6 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ fathom}}\).
Multiply \(23 \text{ fathom} \times \frac{6 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ fathom}}\). The fathom labels cancel, then multiply \(23 \times 6\). Whatever number you get carries the unit "ft".
blocks.sol:
Start with the length you were given and attach a conversion factor that turns fathoms into feet. The factor is \(\frac{6 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ fathom}}\), which equals \(1\) because 6 feet and 1 fathom are the same distance.

Orient it so the unwanted unit cancels. Fathom is on top in the starting amount and on the bottom in the factor, so it cancels.

$$23 \text{ fathom} \times \frac{6 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ fathom}} = 23 \times 6 \text{ ft} = 138 \text{ ft}$$

The fathom labels divided out and feet is the only unit left, exactly what the question asked for. The rope is \(\boxed{138}\) feet long.

blocks.x: In Britain, body weight is often given in a unit called the stone, where 1 stone is exactly 14 pounds. At the check-in desk, a piece of luggage tips the scale at 91 pounds, but the airline's form has a box that wants the weight written in stone. To convert, you build a conversion factor from the fact that \(14 \text{ pounds}\) and \(1 \text{ stone}\) are the same weight, so the ratio \(\frac{1 \text{ stone}}{14 \text{ pounds}}\) equals 1. Orient it so the pounds cancel and stone is left. The result will not be a whole number, and that is fine, since a good method should give an honest answer even when it is not round. What is the weight of the luggage in stone?
hints: You want pounds to cancel and stone to survive. Since 1 stone is 14 pounds, the factor \(\frac{1 \text{ stone}}{14 \text{ pounds}}\) is just a ratio equal to 1. Multiplying by 1 never changes the true weight, it only changes the unit it is written in.

Write \(91 \text{ pounds} \times \frac{1 \text{ stone}}{14 \text{ pounds}}\) and cross out the matching pound units. That leaves the number \(\frac{91}{14}\) carrying the unit stone. Now reduce \(\frac{91}{14}\) to lowest terms by dividing the top and bottom by their greatest common factor.
blocks.sol:
Start with the weight you have and multiply by the conversion factor turned so that pounds sits on the bottom, ready to cancel the pounds you started with.
$$91 \text{ pounds} \times \frac{1 \text{ stone}}{14 \text{ pounds}}$$
The word "pounds" appears once on top and once on the bottom, so it cancels and only stone remains.
$$91 \cancel{\text{ pounds}} \times \frac{1 \text{ stone}}{14 \cancel{\text{ pounds}}} = \frac{91}{14} \text{ stone}$$
Now reduce the fraction. Both 91 and 14 share a factor of 7, since \(91 = 7 \times 13\) and \(14 = 7 \times 2\).
$$\frac{91}{14} = \frac{7 \times 13}{7 \times 2} = \frac{13}{2}$$
So the luggage weighs \(\frac{13}{2}\) stone, which is the same as \(6.5\) stone. The conversion factor did its job perfectly even though the answer landed between two whole numbers.
$$\boxed{13/2}$$

blocks.x: A roadside farm stand bottles its own berry jam and packs the jars into shipping crates, where \(1\) crate holds exactly \(18\) jars. This morning the workers have \(270\) loose jars sitting on the bench, and they want to know how many full crates that fills. Here is the trick worth noticing. The single relationship \(1 \text{ crate} = 18 \text{ jars}\) can be written as a fraction two different ways, either \(\frac{18 \text{ jars}}{1 \text{ crate}}\) or its flip \(\frac{1 \text{ crate}}{18 \text{ jars}}\), and because the top and bottom of each are the same amount, both fractions equal \(1\). Multiplying by either one does not change the real quantity, it only changes the unit it is written in. You are starting in jars and you want to end in crates, so jars has to cancel, which means jars must sit on the bottom of the factor you choose. How many full crates do \(270\) jars make?
hints: You have two factors to pick from, \(\frac{18 \text{ jars}}{1 \text{ crate}}\) and \(\frac{1 \text{ crate}}{18 \text{ jars}}\), and both equal \(1\). The unit you are starting with is jars, and a unit cancels only when it shows up on the top of one fraction and the bottom of another. Since your \(270\) jars is on top, the factor you choose must carry jars on the bottom so the two jars cancel and crates is left behind. Which of the two factors has jars underneath?
blocks.sol: Pick the factor that puts the unwanted unit, jars, on the bottom so it can cancel, which is \(\frac{1 \text{ crate}}{18 \text{ jars}}\). Write the starting amount with its unit and multiply. $$270 \text{ jars} \times \frac{1 \text{ crate}}{18 \text{ jars}}$$ The word jars sits on top in the first piece and on the bottom in the factor, so it cancels and leaves crates standing. $$270 \cancel{\text{ jars}} \times \frac{1 \text{ crate}}{18 \cancel{\text{ jars}}} = \frac{270}{18} \text{ crates}$$ Now just do the arithmetic. $$\frac{270}{18} = 15$$ So \(270\) jars fill \(\boxed{15}\) full crates. Notice the other factor, \(\frac{18 \text{ jars}}{1 \text{ crate}}\), would have left you with units of \(\frac{\text{jars}^2}{\text{crate}}\), which is nonsense, and that is exactly how the units tell you when you have flipped the factor the wrong way.

blocks.x: One fact about two units gives us two conversion factors, the fraction and its flip, and both equal 1 because the top and bottom are the same amount wearing different costumes. So we never guess which way to write it. We put the unit we want gone on the opposite side from where it sits now, so it cancels and leaves the unit we actually wanted standing. And the units check our work for free, since after multiplying we just look at what unit survived. If it is the unit the question asked for, the setup was right, and if the wrong unit is left, we flipped a factor the wrong way.

From this center fact two large fractions branch outward. On the left is the fraction three feet over one yard, with a big equals 1 beside it, and a small note underneath reading use this to turn yards into feet. On the right is the fraction one yard over three feet, with a big equals 1 beside it, and a small note underneath reading use this to turn feet into yards. yards</text><text x="230" y="276" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="10.5" fill="var(--mid)">flip the same true fact to cancel the unit you want gone</text></svg>
blocks.cap: Both fractions are the exact same fact, \(1 \text{ yd} = 3 \text{ ft}\), just wearing reciprocal costumes. Since the top and bottom name the very same length, \(\frac{3 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ yd}} = 1\) and \(\frac{1 \text{ yd}}{3 \text{ ft}} = 1\), and multiplying by either one never changes how much you have, only what units it wears. You grab whichever costume cancels the unit you are trying to shed. To leave yards behind, multiply by the left form so the yards on top and bottom cancel, and to leave feet behind, reach for the right form instead.

blocks.x: A historic racecourse marks its lengths in furlongs, an old unit still used at the track. The groundskeeper wants the distance in feet so the new fencing can be ordered, but the records book has no furlong to feet fact anywhere. It only lists two stepping stones, \(1 \text{ furlong} = 220 \text{ yards}\) and \(1 \text{ yard} = 3 \text{ feet}\). You can still get there by multiplying by \(1\) twice in a row. Start from \(4\) furlongs, multiply by \(\frac{220 \text{ yd}}{1 \text{ furlong}}\) so the furlongs cancel and you land on yards, then multiply by \(\frac{3 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ yd}}\) so the yards cancel and you land on feet. How many feet is \(4\) furlongs?
hints: You cannot jump straight from furlongs to feet, so hop through yards in the middle. Build the first conversion factor from \(1 \text{ furlong} = 220 \text{ yards}\), and orient it so the unit you want to get rid of, furlongs, sits on the bottom. That makes it \(\frac{220 \text{ yd}}{1 \text{ furlong}}\), and since top and bottom are the same length, this fraction equals \(1\), so multiplying by it changes the unit without changing the actual distance.

Now line up the whole chain so each factor cancels the unit the one before it produced. Write \(4 \text{ furlong} \times \frac{220 \text{ yd}}{1 \text{ furlong}} \times \frac{3 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ yd}}\). The furlongs cancel against the first bottom and the yards cancel against the second bottom, leaving feet. Then just multiply the numbers across, \(4 \times 220 \times 3\).
accept: two thousand six hundred and forty
blocks.sol: There is no single furlong to feet factor, so chain two of them through yards. Each conversion factor is a ratio equal to \(1\), oriented so the unwanted unit lands on the bottom and cancels. Lay the chain out left to right. $$4 \text{ furlong} \times \frac{220 \text{ yd}}{1 \text{ furlong}} \times \frac{3 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ yd}}$$ Watch the units cancel, furlong against the first denominator and yard against the second, which leaves only feet. $$4 \cancel{\text{ furlong}} \times \frac{220 \text{ yd}}{1 \cancel{\text{ furlong}}} \times \frac{3 \text{ ft}}{1 \cancel{\text{ yd}}} = 4 \times 220 \times 3 \text{ ft}$$ Now multiply the numbers across. $$4 \times 220 = 880, \qquad 880 \times 3 = 2{,}640$$ So \(4\) furlongs is \(\boxed{2640}\) feet.

blocks.x: Here is what that relay showed us. When no single factor reaches from where you start to where you want to land, you do not need one, you just hop through a middle unit instead, stringing several factors together and letting each one hand off to the next. Every factor is its own little \(1\), so multiplying a whole row of them still multiplies by \(1\), and the real amount never budges no matter how many hops it takes. The only thing to watch is the lineup, set each factor so the unit you are done with sits where it will cancel the one before it, and keep going until every unwanted unit has met its match and only the target unit is left standing at the finish.

A tailor lays out a bolt of cloth that measures \(6\) yards, but the cutting machine only accepts a length typed in inches. The tailor knows two facts, \(1\) yard \(= 3\) feet and \(1\) foot \(= 12\) inches. Nobody wrote down a direct yard-to-inch fact, so you will have to pass through feet on the way. How many inches long is the cloth?
hints: You have no single yard-to-inch factor, so build a bridge through feet. Start with the factor that kills yards. Since \(1\) yard \(= 3\) feet, write it as \(\frac{3 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ yd}}\) so that the "yd" on top of \(6 \text{ yd}\) cancels the "yd" on the bottom of the factor, leaving feet.

Now stack a second factor that kills feet. Use \(\frac{12 \text{ in}}{1 \text{ ft}}\) so "ft" cancels and only "in" survives. The whole chain is \(6 \text{ yd} \times \frac{3 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ yd}} \times \frac{12 \text{ in}}{1 \text{ ft}}\). Multiply the numbers across, \(6 \times 3 \times 12\).
blocks.sol:
There is no yard-to-inch fact, so chain two conversion factors and let the units do the bookkeeping. First turn yards into feet, then feet into inches. Each factor is just a ratio equal to \(1\), oriented so the unwanted unit cancels.

$$6 \text{ yd} \times \frac{3 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ yd}} \times \frac{12 \text{ in}}{1 \text{ ft}}$$

Watch the units cancel. The "yd" on top cancels the "yd" on the bottom, and the "ft" that appears cancels the "ft" below it, leaving only "in".

$$6 \times \frac{3}{1} \times \frac{12}{1} \text{ in} = 6 \times 36 \text{ in} = 216 \text{ in}$$

The cloth is \(\boxed{216}\) inches long.

blocks.cap: Here is the cloth chain laid out as one long product, \(6 \text{ yd} \times \frac{3 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ yd}} \times \frac{12 \text{ in}}{1 \text{ ft}}\). Run your eye across it left to right and watch the units pair off. The yd on the bottom of the first factor finds the yd you started with and the two strike each other out, then the ft on top of that factor finds the ft on the bottom of the next one and they cancel too. Every unit you do not want gets a partner and disappears, and the one unit left standing with no partner, the in on top, is the unit of your answer. That marching line of strike-throughs is your safety check. If a stray unit ever survives where it should not, you know a factor is flipped the wrong way. With the units sorted out you just multiply the surviving numbers, \(6 \times 3 \times 12 = 216\), and read off \(\boxed{216}\) inches.

blocks.x: Here is an old calendar word worth knowing. A fortnight means exactly 14 days, so \(1 \text{ fortnight} = 14 \text{ days}\). A small town runs a lantern festival that lasts exactly 1 fortnight, and the organizers want to print its length in minutes on the closing banner. Nobody has a single fact that turns fortnights straight into minutes, so you build a relay out of stepping stones you do know, \(1 \text{ day} = 24 \text{ hours}\) and \(1 \text{ hour} = 60 \text{ minutes}\). Line up three conversion factors so fortnight cancels, then day cancels, then hour cancels, leaving only minutes standing. How many minutes long is 1 fortnight?
hints: Start with \(1 \text{ fortnight}\) and multiply by a conversion factor that puts \(\text{fortnight}\) on the bottom so it cancels. Since \(1 \text{ fortnight} = 14 \text{ days}\), that first factor is \(\frac{14 \text{ days}}{1 \text{ fortnight}}\), which is just a ratio equal to 1. Now you are holding days, and you need to keep relaying down to minutes.

Chain all three factors in a row, each one cancelling the unit before it. Write \(1 \text{ fortnight} \times \frac{14 \text{ days}}{1 \text{ fortnight}} \times \frac{24 \text{ hr}}{1 \text{ day}} \times \frac{60 \text{ min}}{1 \text{ hr}}\) and multiply the top numbers, \(14 \times 24 \times 60\). Notice \(14 \times 24 = 336\), then \(336 \times 60\).
blocks.sol: Chain the three conversion factors so each unwanted unit cancels in turn, leaving minutes. $$1 \text{ fortnight} \times \frac{14 \text{ days}}{1 \text{ fortnight}} \times \frac{24 \text{ hr}}{1 \text{ day}} \times \frac{60 \text{ min}}{1 \text{ hr}}$$ Watch the relay. The first factor cancels \(\text{fortnight}\), the second cancels \(\text{day}\), and the third cancels \(\text{hr}\), so the only unit left standing is \(\text{min}\). $$1 \times \frac{14}{1} \times \frac{24}{1} \times \frac{60}{1} \text{ min}$$ Multiply the top numbers along the line. First \(14 \times 24 = 336\), then \(336 \times 60 = 20{,}160\). So 1 fortnight is \(\boxed{20160}\) minutes.

blocks.x: In 1999 NASA lost the Mars Climate Orbiter, a spacecraft that cost over 300 million dollars, because of a unit mix-up exactly like the ones in this lesson. One engineering team produced thrust data in pound-force seconds, a US customary unit, while the navigation software was built to expect newton seconds, a metric unit. The numbers were passed along without ever being converted, so each value looked perfectly reasonable while being off by a factor of about 4.45. The unconverted figures nudged the probe far closer to Mars than planned, and it was destroyed in the atmosphere. The whole loss came down to a missing conversion factor, which is the best possible reason to always carry your units and watch them cancel.

At a carnival, one booth sells local play money called sera coins, and it posts a single fixed rate that holds all day long, \(1\) dollar buys \(16\) sera. That rate is really a ratio doing a job. Since \(16\) sera names the exact same value as \(1\) dollar, the fraction \(\frac{16 \text{ sera}}{1 \text{ dollar}}\) is equal to \(1\), so multiplying by it changes the currency costume without changing the value. A traveler hands the booth \(28\) dollars. Set it up as \(28 \text{ dollars} \times \frac{16 \text{ sera}}{1 \text{ dollar}}\) so that "dollars" cancels and "sera" is left standing. How many sera does the traveler receive?
hints: You want the dollars to cancel, so the conversion factor needs dollars on the bottom and the target unit, sera, on top. Write the rate as \(\frac{16 \text{ sera}}{1 \text{ dollar}}\). When you multiply \(28 \text{ dollars}\) by this, the word dollars appears once on top and once on bottom, so it cancels and only sera remains.

Line it up as \(28 \text{ dollars} \times \frac{16 \text{ sera}}{1 \text{ dollar}}\). Cancel the dollars, then the numbers left are \(28 \times 16\) over \(1\). Compute \(28 \times 16\) to get the sera.
blocks.sol: Start with what the traveler has and multiply by the rate written so that dollars cancels. Put sera on top and dollars on the bottom. $$28 \text{ dollars} \times \frac{16 \text{ sera}}{1 \text{ dollar}}$$ The word dollars sits on the top of the first quantity and on the bottom of the factor, so it cancels, the way a matching factor cancels in any fraction. $$28 \, \cancel{\text{dollars}} \times \frac{16 \text{ sera}}{1 \, \cancel{\text{dollar}}} = \frac{28 \times 16}{1} \text{ sera}$$ Now just the arithmetic is left. \(28 \times 16 = 448\), so the traveler receives \(\boxed{448}\) sera.

blocks.x: Here is the thing we just stumbled onto. A rate like 17 pesos for every 1 dollar is really a ratio, and we already know a ratio can be flipped over and still describe the same deal. That makes it a conversion factor exactly like \(\frac{12 \text{ in}}{1 \text{ ft}}\), so we can stand it on its head and multiply, choosing the side that cancels the unit we want gone. Converting money, or a price, or a speed is all one single move, multiply by the rate turned the right way and watch the leftover unit be the one we asked for.

An arcade runs on two fixed exchanges. At the front machine, \(1\) dollar buys \(4\) tokens, so the rate is \(\frac{4 \text{ tokens}}{1 \text{ dollar}}\). At the redemption counter, \(1\) token buys \(3\) prize tickets, so the rate is \(\frac{3 \text{ tickets}}{1 \text{ token}}\). There is no sign anywhere giving a direct dollars-to-tickets rate, so to get from dollars all the way to tickets you have to pass through tokens in the middle. A player walks in and loads \(9\) dollars onto a card. How many prize tickets are those \(9\) dollars worth?
hints: You want to start with \(9 \text{ dollars}\) and end with tickets, so build conversion factors that cancel what you do not want. Each given exchange is a ratio equal to \(1\), and you can flip it either way. Pick the orientation with "dollars" on the bottom for the first factor, \(\frac{4 \text{ tokens}}{1 \text{ dollar}}\), so the dollars divide out.

Dollars alone cannot reach tickets, so chain a second factor that has tokens on the bottom, \(\frac{3 \text{ tickets}}{1 \text{ token}}\). Now write the whole product, \(9 \text{ dollars} \times \frac{4 \text{ tokens}}{1 \text{ dollar}} \times \frac{3 \text{ tickets}}{1 \text{ token}}\), cross out dollars then tokens, and multiply the numbers \(9 \times 4 \times 3\).
blocks.sol: There is no dollars-to-tickets rate given, so chain the two exchanges through tokens. Start with the \(9\) dollars and multiply by a conversion factor that cancels dollars, then by one that cancels tokens. $$9 \text{ dollars} \times \frac{4 \text{ tokens}}{1 \text{ dollar}} \times \frac{3 \text{ tickets}}{1 \text{ token}}$$ The dollars in the first numerator cancel the dollars in the first factor's denominator, and the tokens left behind cancel the tokens in the second factor's denominator. $$9 \, \cancel{\text{dollars}} \times \frac{4 \, \cancel{\text{tokens}}}{1 \, \cancel{\text{dollar}}} \times \frac{3 \text{ tickets}}{1 \, \cancel{\text{token}}}$$ Only tickets survive, which is the target unit. Now multiply the numbers, \(9 \times 4 = 36\) tokens worth of value, then \(36 \times 3 = 108\) tickets. $$9 \times 4 \times 3 = 108 \text{ tickets}$$ So \(9\) dollars is worth \(\boxed{108}\) prize tickets.

blocks.x: Here is one of the trickiest kinds of conversion. A messenger on a bicycle is holding a steady \(45\) miles per hour down a long straight road. That speed is a rate, distance over time, so it carries two units at once, miles on top and hours on the bottom. To restate it in feet per second you have to fix both units, and you will need two facts, \(1 \text{ mile} = 5{,}280 \text{ feet}\) and \(1 \text{ hour} = 3{,}600 \text{ seconds}\). Start by writing the speed as the fraction \(\frac{45 \text{ mi}}{1 \text{ hr}}\). Then attach one conversion factor that turns the miles on top into feet, and another that turns the hours on the bottom into seconds, orienting each one so the unwanted unit cancels. What is the bicycle's speed in feet per second?
hints: A conversion factor is a ratio equal to \(1\), so multiplying by it never changes the speed, only its dress. You have miles on top and you want feet on top, so use \(\frac{5{,}280 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ mi}}\) with the mile on the bottom, ready to cancel the mile in \(\frac{45 \text{ mi}}{1 \text{ hr}}\). Separately, you have hours on the bottom and you want seconds on the bottom, so you will need a factor that puts hours on top to cancel them.

Chain all three pieces together, \(\frac{45 \text{ mi}}{1 \text{ hr}} \times \frac{5{,}280 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ mi}} \times \frac{1 \text{ hr}}{3{,}600 \text{ s}}\). The miles cancel and the hours cancel, leaving feet over seconds. Now just the numbers remain, \(\frac{45 \times 5{,}280}{3{,}600}\), which is \(\frac{237{,}600}{3{,}600}\).
blocks.sol: Write the speed as a fraction, then multiply by two conversion factors, each equal to \(1\), arranged so the unwanted units cancel. $$\frac{45 \text{ mi}}{1 \text{ hr}} \times \frac{5{,}280 \text{ ft}}{1 \text{ mi}} \times \frac{1 \text{ hr}}{3{,}600 \text{ s}}$$ The \(\cancel{\text{mi}}\) on top cancels the \(\cancel{\text{mi}}\) on the bottom, and the \(\cancel{\text{hr}}\) on the bottom cancels the \(\cancel{\text{hr}}\) on top, so what survives is feet over seconds, exactly the unit we want. $$\frac{45 \times 5{,}280}{3{,}600} \text{ ft/s} = \frac{237{,}600}{3{,}600} \text{ ft/s} = 66 \text{ ft/s}$$ A conversion factor is just a ratio doing a job, and chaining two of them lets us fix both halves of a rate at once. \(\boxed{66}\)

blocks.x: So that is what tripped me up about a speed. It is not one quantity sitting alone, it is a ratio with one unit riding on top and a different unit underneath, so a single conversion factor only ever fixes one of them. To rebuild both, I have to multiply by two factors at once, one aimed at the top unit and one aimed at the bottom unit, each flipped so its unwanted unit lands across from a matching copy and cancels. The moment the old top and the old bottom have both crossed out, whatever new-top-over-new-bottom is left standing is my converted rate.

Density is a rate, the grams of stuff packed into each unit of volume. A bottling line uses a sweet syrup with a density of \(1.5\) grams per milliliter, written \(\frac{1.5 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mL}}\). One mixing tank holds \(4\) liters of this syrup. Since the density is measured per milliliter, you first need the volume in milliliters, and \(1\) liter \(= 1000\) milliliters. Multiplying a volume by a density gives a mass, because the volume units cancel and grams are left behind. How many grams of syrup does the tank hold?
hints: The density \(\frac{1.5 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mL}}\) is built for milliliters, but your volume is in liters. Before you can use it, turn \(4\) liters into milliliters with a conversion factor that cancels liters, oriented as \(\frac{1000 \text{ mL}}{1 \text{ liter}}\) so "liter" is on the bottom.

Build the full chain \(4 \text{ liters} \times \frac{1000 \text{ mL}}{1 \text{ liter}} \times \frac{1.5 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mL}}\). The first factor cancels liters and the second cancels milliliters, leaving grams. Now just multiply the numbers, \(4 \times 1000 \times 1.5\).
blocks.sol: Start with the volume and chain on two conversion factors, each oriented so the unwanted unit sits on the bottom and cancels. First send liters to milliliters, then let the density turn milliliters into grams. $$4 \text{ liters} \times \frac{1000 \text{ mL}}{1 \text{ liter}} \times \frac{1.5 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mL}}$$ Watch the units fall away. The "liter" on top cancels the "liter" on the bottom, and then the "mL" cancels the "mL", leaving grams. $$4 \, \cancel{\text{liters}} \times \frac{1000 \, \cancel{\text{mL}}}{1 \, \cancel{\text{liter}}} \times \frac{1.5 \text{ g}}{1 \, \cancel{\text{mL}}}$$ With only grams remaining, multiply the numbers across the top. \(4 \times 1000 = 4000\) milliliters, and \(4000 \times 1.5 = 6000\) grams. The tank holds \(\boxed{6000}\) grams of syrup.

blocks.x: A graphic designer printed a square sticker that covers an area of \(12\) square inches, and a client overseas wants to know its area in square centimeters. The only fact the designer has is the linear relationship \(1 \text{ in} = 2.5 \text{ cm}\). It is tempting to just multiply \(12\) by \(2.5\), but think carefully about what a square inch really is. It is a little square that measures \(1\) inch along each side, and a square centimeter measures \(1\) cm along each side. How many square centimeters is the sticker?
hints: A conversion factor is a ratio equal to \(1\). Here the relationship \(1 \text{ in} = 2.5 \text{ cm}\) gives the factor \(\frac{2.5 \text{ cm}}{1 \text{ in}}\). But the unit you are converting is square inches, which is \(\text{in} \times \text{in}\), so a single \(\frac{\text{cm}}{\text{in}}\) only cancels one of the two inches. What happens to the leftover inch?

Multiply by the factor \(\frac{2.5 \text{ cm}}{1 \text{ in}}\) twice, once for each side of the little square. Watch both inch units cancel and two centimeter units appear. Then the number part is \(12 \times 2.5 \times 2.5\), so the linear factor \(2.5\) is squared.
blocks.sol:
A square inch is \(1 \text{ in} \times 1 \text{ in}\), so to convert it you must convert each side from inches to centimeters. That means the conversion factor \(\frac{2.5 \text{ cm}}{1 \text{ in}}\) gets used twice, once for each inch. Start with the area and carry the units along.

$$12 \text{ in}^2 \times \frac{2.5 \text{ cm}}{1 \text{ in}} \times \frac{2.5 \text{ cm}}{1 \text{ in}}$$

Write \(\text{in}^2\) as \(\text{in} \times \text{in}\) and watch the cancelling. One \(\text{in}\) on top cancels with the \(\text{in}\) in the first factor, and the other \(\text{in}\) cancels with the \(\text{in}\) in the second factor.

$$12 \, \cancel{\text{in}} \, \cancel{\text{in}} \times \frac{2.5 \text{ cm}}{1 \, \cancel{\text{in}}} \times \frac{2.5 \text{ cm}}{1 \, \cancel{\text{in}}}$$

Both inches are gone and two centimeter units are left over, which multiply to \(\text{cm}^2\). The numbers are \(12 \times 2.5 \times 2.5\). The linear factor \(2.5\) appears twice, so it is squared, \(2.5 \times 2.5 = 6.25\). Then \(12 \times 6.25 = 75\). The sticker is \(\boxed{75}\) square centimeters.

<svg viewBox="0 0 420 360" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A single square labeled one square inch, with its width and its height each annotated 1 in = 2.5 cm. The square is divided by a grid into 2.5 by 2.5 centimeter units, so you can count 6.25 small square-centimeter cells inside, four full one-by-one cells, four half cells along the right and bottom edges, and one quarter cell in the corner, which add up to 6.25 square centimeters. To the right the arithmetic reads 1 sq in equals 2.5 cm times 2.5 cm equals 6.25 sq cm, with the two 2.5 factors circled to show the same linear factor 2.5 is used once for the width and once for the height, so it appears twice and gets squared. A caption explains that converting an area applies the linear factor twice, so it is squared.">
